questions.stem:
A company is migrating from an on-premises data warehouse to a cloud-based data platform. Their current system couples storage and compute in a single appliance, making it expensive to scale query capacity without also paying for more storage.

Which statement best describes the core architectural shift the modern data stack enables?
options: Centralise all data processing in a single, vertically-scaled compute node to eliminate data movement overhead.
Separate storage from compute so each can scale independently, letting different tools query the same underlying data without copying it.
Replace the database layer entirely with streaming pipelines so that all data is processed in real time as it arrives.
Consolidate ingestion, transformation, and serving into one managed service to reduce the number of tools teams must operate.
questions.explanation: The defining breakthrough of the modern data stack is decoupling storage from compute. Cloud object stores like ADLS Gen2 hold all data as files accessible to any authorised compute engine without copying it. Each engine spins up on demand and scales to zero when idle, so cost tracks actual usage rather than reserved capacity.

questions.stem:
A compliance team requires that raw audit logs be retained for 7 years. The logs are accessed only for quarterly regulatory reviews and must be retrievable within minutes when needed.

Which ADLS Gen2 access tier is most appropriate?
options: Hot — compliance requirements mean data must always be instantly available, and Hot provides the lowest possible access latency.
Cool — infrequent access with the same millisecond availability as Hot, at a lower storage cost per GB.
Cold — rarely accessed data with instant millisecond retrieval at a lower storage cost than Cool, suited for quarterly or less-frequent access patterns.
Archive — the lowest possible storage cost; rehydration is fast enough to meet a within-minutes SLA.
questions.explanation: Cold tier provides millisecond access (no rehydration required) at a lower cost than Cool, making it ideal for data accessed a few times per year. Archive requires hours of rehydration, violating the within-minutes requirement.

questions.stem: Statement: Azure Data Factory is designed to perform complex data transformation logic directly within its pipelines. Building Silver-layer cleansing and enrichment jobs inside ADF Data Flow activities is the recommended pattern for a modern Azure medallion architecture.
questions.explanation: FALSE. ADF is an orchestration and ingestion tool, not a heavy transformation engine. Data Flow activities exist but are expensive and limited. The recommended pattern delegates transformation to Databricks or dbt — ADF triggers the jobs, it does not do the work itself.

questions.stem:
A large organisation has a central data team that owns all pipelines for all domains. Business teams complain that their data requests take months and the central team is a bottleneck. Leadership asks for the single most impactful change a data mesh adoption would make.

Which principle is the most direct response to this bottleneck?
options: Self-serve data platform — build internal tooling so that domain teams can create and deploy pipelines without needing central engineering support.
Domain-oriented decentralised data ownership — make each domain team accountable for producing and maintaining its own data products.
Federated computational governance — apply global data policies automatically so that no central team is needed to enforce compliance.
Data as a product — require each domain to treat their data outputs with the same quality standards as customer-facing software.
questions.explanation: The bottleneck described is a centralised ownership problem. Domain-oriented decentralised ownership directly addresses it by shifting accountability to the teams closest to the data. Self-serve tooling and federated governance are enablers, not the primary principle that resolves the bottleneck.

questions.stem:
A SQL Server query references the same CTE three times. A developer expects the CTE to execute once and cache its result. After profiling, they see the underlying subquery executed three times.

Which statement correctly explains this behaviour?
options: SQL Server guarantees every CTE is materialised once per query execution, regardless of how many times it is referenced.
SQL Server treats the CTE as a macro substitution, inlining its definition at each reference point; the optimiser may execute the underlying subquery up to three times.
SQL Server materialises a CTE only when it is referenced more than twice; a CTE referenced exactly once or twice is always evaluated inline.
SQL Server uses a streaming iterator to pipe the CTE's output to all three reference points simultaneously, so the source data is read exactly once without caching.
questions.explanation: In most SQL engines including SQL Server, a CTE is a named subquery — a macro, not a cache. The optimiser inlines the CTE definition at each reference point and may choose to execute it multiple times. If materialisation is required, use a temp table or a staging layer.

questions.stem:
A data team commits that the orders pipeline will deliver data by 7am each day with 99.5% monthly uptime. They track this target internally and alert on breaches before stakeholders notice.

What is the correct term for this specific, measurable internal performance target?
options: Service Level Agreement (SLA) — the formal contract in which this commitment is recorded and agreed between the team and its stakeholders.
Service Level Objective (SLO) — the specific, measurable performance target the team monitors and is held accountable to meeting.
Data contract — a machine-enforceable specification of schema, freshness windows, and quality rules agreed between a producer and consumer.
Data quality threshold — a runtime assertion applied by the pipeline to flag or quarantine individual records that breach acceptable quality bounds.
questions.explanation: An SLO is the specific measurable target (99.5% uptime, delivered by 7am). An SLA is the broader agreement that contains SLOs and defines consequences for breach. Data contracts and quality thresholds operate at the record level, not the pipeline level.

questions.stem: True or False: A dead letter queue and a data archive serve the same purpose — both hold records that are no longer part of the active pipeline but must be retained for future reference.
questions.explanation: FALSE. A dead letter queue holds records that failed processing — they are quarantined for investigation and potential reprocessing. An archive holds records that completed processing successfully but are no longer needed in hot storage. Their purposes, contents, and SLAs are entirely different.

questions.stem:
A CI/CD pipeline fails immediately on a YAML config file with a parse error. The file looks correct visually in the editor.

What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The file contains unquoted boolean values such as true or false. Fix: wrap these values in single quotes so the parser treats them as strings.
The file uses tab characters for indentation instead of spaces. Fix: replace every tab with the appropriate number of spaces — YAML strictly prohibits tabs as indentation.
The file uses // comments copied from a JSON schema. Fix: replace // with # which is the only YAML comment syntax.
The file mixes single-quoted and double-quoted strings in the same document. Fix: standardise on one quoting style throughout.
questions.explanation: YAML prohibits tab characters for indentation — only spaces are valid. Editors that render tabs and spaces identically will hide this problem visually. It is the most common cause of YAML parse errors that look correct to the eye. Always configure your editor to show whitespace in YAML files.

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs the following code inside a Databricks notebook connected to a 10-worker cluster:

import polars as pl
df = pl.read_delta("abfss://gold@…/sales")

Statement: This code distributes the Delta table read across all 10 worker nodes, leveraging the full cluster for faster ingestion.
questions.explanation: FALSE. Polars is a single-node library. It runs entirely on the driver node of the Databricks cluster — the 10 workers are completely unused. For distributed reads across a cluster, use PySpark or Spark SQL. Polars is excellent for single-node workloads but is the wrong tool when you need distributed execution.

questions.stem:
A Spark job with 200 partitions completes in 45 minutes but one task consistently runs for 44 minutes while all others finish in under 1 minute.

What is the most likely cause?
options: The cluster has too few executor cores. Adding more cores would distribute the work across more tasks and bring that one slow task below one minute.
The Delta table has too many small files. Running OPTIMIZE would consolidate them into larger files, reducing the number of tasks and balancing the load.
The data is skewed on the shuffle key. A dominant key value causes one partition to receive almost all the data, leaving one task vastly larger than the others.
The driver node is underpowered. Because the driver schedules all tasks and collects results, a slow driver creates a bottleneck that stalls the entire job.
questions.explanation: When one task takes nearly as long as the entire job, data skew is the diagnosis — one partition is receiving a disproportionate share of the data due to a dominant shuffle key value. Adding cores doesn't help because the bottleneck is data volume in one partition, not parallelism. Fix with salting, AQE skew hints, or repartitioning.

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes the following PySpark code in a Databricks notebook:

df2 = df.filter(col('status') == 'active')
df3 = df2.withColumn('tax', col('amount') * 0.2)
df3.show(5)

Statement: The filter and withColumn transformations execute immediately when each line runs, and show(5) simply displays the already-computed result.
questions.explanation: FALSE. PySpark uses lazy evaluation. The filter and withColumn calls build a logical plan but execute nothing. The actual computation is triggered only by the show(5) action. This design allows Spark's Catalyst optimiser to reorder, combine, and optimise the full plan before any data is read or processed.

questions.stem:
A customer moved from the North region to the South region. Your fact table contains historical sales linked to this customer. Business stakeholders require that historical sales continue to show the region the customer was in at the time of the sale.

Which SCD type is correct?
options: SCD Type 1 — overwrite the region column in place; historical queries automatically see the updated value.
SCD Type 3 — add a previous_region column alongside the current value so analysts can see both regions.
SCD Type 2 — insert a new row for the customer, setting effective_date, expiry_date, and a current-row flag, so historical facts join to the row that was active at sale time.
SCD Type 0 — retain the original region and never update it; historical integrity is preserved by default.
questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 is the correct pattern for preserving history. A new row is inserted with the new attribute value and effective/expiry dates. Historical fact rows still reference the old surrogate key, which points to the old row — so they correctly show the region at time of sale. Type 1 overwrites and destroys history. Type 3 only tracks one prior value.

questions.stem: Statement: DirectQuery mode is the best default choice for Power BI semantic models because it always shows the most up-to-date data from the source, eliminates the need for scheduled refreshes, and performs equally well as Import mode for large datasets.
questions.explanation: FALSE. DirectQuery queries the source on every interaction, making report performance highly dependent on source system speed. It is appropriate when data freshness is critical and the source can handle the query load. For most large datasets, Import mode is significantly faster for end users. DirectQuery also has DAX and feature limitations not present in Import mode.

questions.stem:
You have a Power BI report with a slicer on dim_region. You need a measure that always shows total sales across ALL regions, regardless of what the user selects in the region slicer.

Which DAX expression is correct?
options: CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT(dim_date, dim_date[Year]))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(dim_region))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLSELECTED(dim_region))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS())
questions.explanation: ALL(dim_region) removes all filters from the region dimension, so the measure always returns the grand total regardless of slicer selection. ALLSELECTED preserves the current slicer context. REMOVEFILTERS() without arguments would remove ALL filters from ALL tables, which is too broad. ALLEXCEPT addresses a different table entirely.

questions.stem:
A data team adopts Scrum with two-week sprints and story-point estimation. After three months the team consistently fails to complete sprint commitments and velocity is unpredictable.

What is the most accurate explanation for why Scrum struggles in data engineering contexts?
options: Data pipelines require more unit tests than software features, so velocity is lower than story-point estimates assume.
Scrum ceremonies consume too much time on large data teams, leaving insufficient sprint capacity for actual pipeline work.
Data work is inherently exploratory — the full scope and edge cases of a pipeline only become visible once you examine the source data, making upfront sprint commitments structurally unreliable.
Story-point estimation is only reliable for UI features; data engineers should use hours-based estimation to get accurate forecasts.
questions.explanation: The core tension is that Scrum asks you to commit to a scope at sprint start, but data engineering scope is discovered during the work. Source data quality, schema surprises, and edge cases are unknowable until you touch the data. This is a structural mismatch, not a team or tooling problem. Kanban or Shape Up tend to work better for data teams.

questions.stem: Statement: When Azure Data Factory is connected to a Git repository, clicking the Publish button in the ADF Studio UI is the correct way to promote changes from a feature branch to the collaboration branch before merging to main.
questions.explanation: FALSE. In Git mode, Publish deploys the collaboration branch directly to the live ADF service — it is a production deployment action, not a branch promotion. Changes should be promoted between branches via Git pull requests, exactly as with normal code. Clicking Publish without a proper PR review bypasses change control and deploys directly to production.

questions.stem:
A VP of Marketing asks your team to prove whether promotional campaigns drive incremental sales. There are 14 campaign types across 6 channels and 3 years of data.

Which approach best represents MVP thinking?
options: Build a full campaign analytics platform covering all 14 campaign types, with drill-through by region, channel, customer segment, and promotion tier.
Ingest all available marketing and sales source systems into the data warehouse so the team has comprehensive data before scoping analysis.
Identify the single highest-spend campaign type, pull pre- and post-campaign sales for matched customer cohorts, and produce a one-page comparison showing incremental uplift or its absence.
Deliver a self-service dashboard where stakeholders can slice campaign data themselves, reducing future ad-hoc requests to the data team.
questions.explanation: MVP thinking asks: what is the smallest deliverable that answers the core question? The core question is whether campaigns drive incremental sales. One campaign type, one clean comparison, one page. If the answer is no, scope expansion was premature. If yes, you have a proven template to scale. Options A and B build infrastructure before validating the question.

questions.stem:
You need to present data quality findings to a CFO who has 10 minutes. You have 20 slides of analysis. The CFO's primary concern is business risk, not methodology.

Which communication framework should you apply?
options: The So What framework — each slide should end with an explicit so what statement so the CFO knows why each piece of data matters.
The Pyramid Principle — lead with the recommendation and its business implication, then provide supporting evidence for those who want the detail.
Audience matching — a CFO needs financial framing, so all metrics should be translated into cost-per-incident figures before the deck is presented.
Data storytelling — build a narrative arc from problem to analysis to insight so the CFO follows the journey to your conclusion.
questions.explanation: The Pyramid Principle (Barbara Minto) puts the conclusion first, then supporting arguments, then detail. This is correct for executives who need to make decisions quickly and may not have time for the full analysis. Leading with the recommendation lets them engage on what matters to them and stop when they have enough context.

questions.stem: Statement: Delivering a high-quality training programme to all users in the week before a new data product goes live is sufficient to drive adoption, because users who understand the tool will choose to use it.
questions.explanation: FALSE. Understanding a tool does not create the habit of using it. Adoption requires sustained behavioural change: embedded champions, workflow integration, visible quick wins, and ongoing reinforcement after go-live. One-time pre-launch training addresses knowledge, not motivation, habit, or organisational friction. Adoption curves consistently show post-launch support matters more than pre-launch training.

questions.stem:
A legal team uses an LLM to summarise case documents. The model consistently produces summaries that are fluent and plausible but contain fabricated case citations that do not exist.

What is the correct explanation for this behaviour?
options: LLMs are trained on internet text that contains factual errors, so the model memorises incorrect facts and reproduces them.
The model's context window is too small to hold the entire document, so it fabricates content to fill gaps in its reading.

LLMs generate text by predicting the most plausible next token — a process optimised for fluency and coherence, not factual accuracy — so confident-sounding fabrication is an inherent output of the generation mechanism itself.
The model's temperature was set too high, causing it to sample from lower-probability tokens and produce less accurate content.
questions.explanation: Hallucination is a direct consequence of how LLMs work: next-token prediction optimises for plausible continuation, not ground truth. The model has no mechanism to distinguish 'I know this' from 'this sounds right'. Lowering temperature reduces variety but not hallucination. Context window size affects completeness, not fabrication of citations.

questions.stem:
A RAG system retrieves relevant document chunks to answer questions about a technical specification. Users report that answers are often retrieved correctly but are incomplete — the model answers part of the question and stops.

What does this indicate about the chunking strategy?
options: Smaller chunks are always better because they produce more precise embeddings; the incomplete answers signal a generation problem, not a chunking problem.

Chunk size controls the precision-completeness tradeoff: smaller chunks retrieve more precisely but may lack the surrounding context the model needs to answer fully; larger chunks include more context but dilute the embedding signal, reducing retrieval accuracy.
The correct fix is to use larger chunks exclusively — retrieval precision can always be recovered by increasing the number of chunks retrieved (top-k).
Chunk size has no direct effect on retrieval quality; the issue is that the embedding model needs to be replaced with a domain-specific fine-tuned version.
questions.explanation: Incomplete answers with correct retrieval is the classic symptom of chunks that are too small. The right chunk is retrieved but it doesn't contain enough surrounding context for the model to answer fully. Increasing chunk size or using overlapping windows captures more context per chunk. This is a tuning tradeoff, not a binary choice.

questions.stem: Statement: The ReAct (Reason + Act) pattern improves agent reliability over pure chain-of-thought prompting because interleaving reasoning steps with actual tool calls grounds each subsequent reasoning step in real observations rather than compounding assumptions.
questions.explanation: TRUE. Pure chain-of-thought reasons in isolation and can compound errors across steps. ReAct alternates between Thought (reasoning) and Action (tool call), then incorporates the Observation (tool result) into the next reasoning step. This grounding in actual data reduces hallucination chains and produces more reliable multi-step agent behaviour.

questions.stem:
A pipeline ingests daily transaction data, validates schema, routes records to Bronze/Silver layers, and at the end generates an anomaly summary email using an LLM. The LLM has no ability to alter pipeline decisions or route data differently.

Where does this pipeline sit on the agentic spectrum?
options: AI-augmented — the LLM assists a deterministic pipeline by providing an analytical layer, but the core logic is deterministic and the LLM cannot alter execution decisions or pipeline structure.
AI-autonomous — because the LLM generates content that triggers a human action, it is driving the outcome of the pipeline process.
AI-autonomous — the LLM is making decisions about which records are suspicious, which is an execution decision that a deterministic rule would otherwise make.
Fully agentic — any pipeline that uses an LLM to produce an output is by definition operating in autonomous mode.
questions.explanation: AI-augmented means the LLM is a component in a deterministic pipeline, not the decision-maker. Here the pipeline's structure, routing, and execution are fixed — the LLM only interprets output at the end. For a pipeline to be agentic, the LLM must be able to make decisions that alter what the pipeline does next.

questions.stem:
A data engineering team ingests raw IoT telemetry logs into ADLS Gen2.
        After the first 30 days, the logs are almost never queried — but compliance investigators
        must be able to retrieve specific records within minutes if requested.
        Logs are retained for five years.
        Which ADLS access tier is most cost-effective for logs older than 30 days?
options: Hot — compliance requirements mean data must always be instantly available, and Hot provides the lowest possible access latency.
Cool — infrequent access with the same millisecond availability as Hot, at a lower storage cost per GB.
Cold — rarely accessed data with instant millisecond retrieval at a lower storage cost than Cool, suited for quarterly or less-frequent access patterns.
Archive — the lowest possible storage cost; rehydration is fast enough to meet a "within minutes" SLA.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: Cold tier is designed for data accessed at most a few times per year. Like Hot and Cool, Cold reads return data immediately — no rehydration step. Its storage cost is roughly half that of Cool, making it significantly cheaper over a five-year retention window. Crucially, it still satisfies the "within minutes" retrieval SLA because access latency is measured in milliseconds, not hours. Why the distractors fail: A — Hot : Optimised for data accessed daily or weekly. Its higher storage rate wastes budget on data that is almost never read. B — Cool : A reasonable second choice for monthly-access data, but its storage rate sits between Hot and Cold. For data accessed only a few times per year over five years, Cool over-prices the storage tier relative to Cold. D — Archive : Archive has the lowest storage cost but requires rehydration before data becomes readable. Standard rehydration takes up to 15 hours; high-priority takes up to 1 hour. Neither meets a "within minutes" SLA. Practical takeaway: Map your retrieval SLA first — if minutes matter, Archive is eliminated immediately. Then compare Cool vs Cold storage rates against expected access frequency to find the economic optimum for your retention period.

questions.stem:
A large enterprise has a single central data team responsible for all data pipelines
        across fifteen business domains. The team is perpetually backlogged, data requests
        take weeks to fulfil, and domain teams frequently complain that the pipelines do not
        accurately reflect their business logic.
        Which Data Mesh principle most directly addresses this organisational failure?
options: Self-serve data platform — build internal tooling so that domain teams can create and deploy pipelines without needing central engineering support.
Domain-oriented decentralised data ownership — make each domain team accountable for producing and maintaining its own data products.
Federated computational governance — apply global data policies automatically so that no central team is needed to enforce compliance.
Data as a product — require domain teams to treat their data with the same SLA and documentation rigour as a customer-facing software product.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: The scenario describes a classic centralised-team bottleneck. The principle of domain-oriented decentralised ownership moves accountability from the central team to the domain teams themselves — the Sales domain owns the sales pipeline because domain engineers understand the data's business rules, edge cases, and freshness requirements better than any central team ever could. Why the distractors fail: A — Self-serve platform : This principle reduces engineering friction for domain teams, but it presupposes that ownership has already been transferred. A self-serve platform without an ownership shift still leaves the central team responsible for correctness. It enables decentralisation; it doesn't cause it. C — Federated governance : Governance ensures consistent policies across all domains. It is a coordination mechanism, not a solution to the backlog or accuracy problem. Governance operates after ownership is established. D — Data as a product : This principle establishes quality standards for domain-produced data. It operates downstream of ownership: you must first assign ownership before you can hold owners accountable to product standards. Practical takeaway: In a strategy or interview conversation, distinguish the root cause (centralised ownership bottleneck) from the enabling principles (self-serve platform, governance). The structural answer is ownership decentralisation.

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes a complex query in SQL Server containing a CTE named
        customer_spend. The CTE is referenced three times in the main
        query body — once in a filter, once in a join, and once in a subquery.
        The engineer assumes the CTE will be computed once and its result cached
        for all three references.
        Which statement most accurately describes what SQL Server actually does with this CTE?
options: SQL Server guarantees every CTE is materialised once per query execution, regardless of how many times it is referenced — the result is stored in a work table and reused.
SQL Server treats the CTE as a macro substitution, inlining its definition at each reference point; the optimiser may execute the underlying subquery up to three times.
SQL Server materialises a CTE only when it is referenced more than twice; a CTE referenced exactly once or twice is always evaluated inline.
SQL Server uses a streaming iterator to pipe the CTE's output to all three reference points simultaneously, so the source data is read exactly once without caching.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: In SQL Server (and Spark SQL), a CTE is a named query expression — not a materialised result. The query optimiser treats it as a macro: it substitutes the CTE's definition inline wherever the name appears and then optimises the expanded query as a whole. If customer_spend is referenced three times, the engine can — and often will — execute the underlying subquery three times. Why the distractors fail: A — No automatic single-materialisation guarantee exists in T-SQL's standard CTE semantics. The "computed once" belief is the most common CTE misconception among intermediate SQL writers. C — There is no reference-count threshold that triggers automatic materialisation in SQL Server. This rule does not exist. D — "Streaming iterator piping to multiple references simultaneously" is not how SQL Server executes query plans. Practical takeaway: When a CTE is expensive and referenced multiple times, replace it with a temp table ( SELECT … INTO #temp ). Temp tables are always materialised, give the optimiser a row-count statistic to work with, and can be indexed — making subsequent joins significantly faster on large datasets.

questions.stem:
A data platform team agrees with its business stakeholders that 99.5% of all
        fact table records will be available in the reporting layer within four hours
        of their source event timestamp. When the platform misses this target, it
        triggers a formal incident review.
        The specific, measurable target of "99.5% within four hours" is best described as a:
options: Service Level Agreement (SLA) — the formal contract or document in which this commitment is recorded and agreed between the team and its stakeholders.
Service Level Objective (SLO) — the specific, measurable performance target the team monitors and is held accountable to meeting.
Data contract — a machine-enforceable specification of schema, freshness windows, and quality rules agreed between a data producer and consumer.
Data quality threshold — a runtime assertion applied by the pipeline to flag or quarantine individual records that breach acceptable quality bounds.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: An SLO is the specific, numeric target a team commits to — "99.5% of records available within four hours." SLOs are what teams monitor on dashboards, and missing one triggers a review or incident. They are the measurable obligations that drive engineering decisions about reliability, retry logic, and alerting thresholds. Why the distractors fail: A — An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is the broader formal document or contract between the team and its stakeholders. The SLA contains one or more SLOs. Confusing the container (SLA) with the metric inside it (SLO) is the most common error on this topic. C — A data contract is a producer/consumer agreement specified in a structured format that defines schema, ownership, expected freshness, and quality rules. It is not a single numeric reliability target. D — A data quality threshold is a row-level check run inside the pipeline. It operates on individual records and routes bad ones to a dead letter queue. It is not a service-reliability metric. Practical takeaway: When writing a data platform charter, keep SLA and SLO separate in your language. The SLA is the signed agreement; the SLO is the number your monitoring alerts on. Stakeholders care about SLAs; engineers operate on SLOs.

questions.stem:
True or False: A dead letter queue and a data archive serve
        the same purpose — both hold records that are no longer part of the active
        pipeline but must be retained for future reference.
questions.explanation: False. A dead letter queue (DLQ) and a data archive serve fundamentally different purposes and should never be conflated. A dead letter queue is a failure-isolation mechanism. It captures records that the pipeline could not process successfully — due to schema violations, parse errors, null constraint failures, or unexpected formats. Records in the DLQ represent active pipeline problems; they are quarantined so that one bad record cannot block the entire pipeline. A DLQ should always be monitored with alerting — a growing DLQ is a signal that something is broken. A data archive (e.g., ADLS Archive tier) stores legitimate, successfully-processed historical records that are rarely queried but must be retained for compliance, audit, or analytical backfill. The data is valid — it is stored cheaply because it is correct and complete, not because it failed. The surface similarity — both are "not in the active query path" — masks opposite intents: a DLQ signals "investigate this failure"; an archive signals "store this correct history cheaply." Practical takeaway: Design both explicitly. Route pipeline failures to a DLQ with an Azure Monitor alert on queue depth. Route aged-out processed data to an archive with a lifecycle policy. If they share the same storage container, you will struggle to distinguish broken records from valid history during an audit.

questions.stem:
A data engineer pastes the following snippet into a dbt project's
        dbt_project.yml and runs dbt parse.
        The command fails with the error:
        yaml.scanner.ScannerError: while scanning for the next token
        found character '\t' that cannot start any token
        What is the root cause, and which fix resolves it?
options: The file contains an unquoted boolean value such as true or false . Fix: wrap these values in single quotes so the parser treats them as strings.
The file uses tab characters for indentation instead of spaces. Fix: replace every tab with the appropriate number of spaces — YAML strictly prohibits tabs as indentation.
The file uses // comments copied from a JSON schema. Fix: replace // with # , which is the only YAML comment syntax.
The file mixes single-quoted and double-quoted strings in the same document. Fix: standardise on one quoting style throughout the file.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: The error message explicitly names the offending character — '\t' is the tab character. YAML's specification forbids tab characters as indentation; only space characters (U+0020) are valid. This rule exists because tab width is ambiguous: different editors render a tab as 2, 4, or 8 spaces, making the structural depth of a mapping undefined. Why the distractors fail: A — Unquoted true , false , null , and numeric literals are legal YAML. They can cause downstream type mismatches in tools that expect strings, but they never produce a ScannerError: found character '\t' . C — YAML does not support // comments, and the parser would raise a different error if one appeared. But the error message here explicitly identifies a tab character, not a slash. D — YAML permits both single and double quotes in the same document. Mixing them is not a syntax error. Practical takeaway: Configure your editor to "indent with spaces" for all YAML files, and add a .editorconfig entry ( indent_style = space ) so that every contributor's editor respects the rule automatically. Tools like yamllint catch tabs before they reach CI.

questions.stem:
A Spark job reading a 400 GB Delta table completes, but takes three times
        longer than expected. Inspecting the Spark UI shows that Stage 3 has
        200 tasks: 199 finish within 45 seconds, but one task runs for 28 minutes
        and processes roughly 90% of the total data for that stage.
        What is the most likely root cause?
options: The cluster has too few executor cores. Adding more cores would distribute the work across more tasks and bring that one slow task below one minute.
The Delta table has too many small files. Running OPTIMIZE would consolidate them into larger files, reducing the number of tasks and balancing the load.

The data is skewed on the shuffle key. A dominant key value (e.g., a single customer_id or status = "active") causes one partition to receive almost all the data, leaving one task vastly larger than the others.
The driver node is underpowered. Because the driver schedules all tasks and collects results, a slow driver creates a bottleneck that stalls the entire job.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: The diagnostic signature — one task taking 28 minutes while 199 peers finish in 45 seconds, processing 90% of the stage's data — is the textbook pattern for data skew. During a shuffle operation, Spark routes all records sharing the same key to the same partition. If one key value dominates the dataset, its partition becomes enormous and the corresponding task cannot be parallelised further. Adding more executors doesn't help: that one giant partition still maps to one task on one core. Why the distractors fail: A — More cores create more tasks, but skew is a key-distribution problem. The skewed partition still maps to a single task regardless of cluster size. B — Small files create many tiny tasks (a different pathology visible as hundreds of fast 1-second tasks). They don't cause one task to process 90% of the data. D — The driver handles scheduling, not data processing. A slow driver produces uniform latency across all tasks, not a single-task straggler consuming 90% of the work. Practical fixes for skew: (1) Enable Spark's AQE with spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true — it auto-splits large partitions. (2) Add a salt column to the skewed key, distribute the dominant value across multiple partitions, then aggregate twice. (3) Use a broadcast join if the smaller side of a skewed join is small enough to fit in executor memory.

questions.stem:
A dbt model reads 500 million rows of raw event data, deduplicates them,
        and produces a cleaned dataset. A full rebuild takes 45 minutes. Downstream
        models only ever need the new records appended since the last run —
        they never query historical data differently based on past corrections.
        The team's goal is to keep daily dbt runs well under 10 minutes.
        Which dbt materialization should this model use?
options: view — dbt creates a SQL view; no data is stored. Downstream models always compute against the full 500 M-row source at query time.
table — dbt drops and recreates the full table on every run. The dataset stays fresh but every run costs the full 45 minutes.
incremental — dbt inserts (or merges) only the rows added or changed since the last run, so subsequent runs process a small daily slice rather than the full 500 M rows.
ephemeral — dbt inlines this model as a CTE inside every downstream query. No table is created, and the 500 M-row transformation runs inside each downstream query at execution time.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: incremental is designed exactly for this pattern. On the first run dbt builds the full table. On every subsequent run it generates a SQL statement that processes only rows newer than the high-water mark and inserts or merges them into the existing table. A 45-minute full rebuild becomes a sub-10-minute daily append. Why the distractors fail: A — view: A view stores no data. Every downstream query re-runs the full 500 M-row transformation at query time — the worst possible choice for a heavy, repeatedly-queried dataset. B — table: A table materializes the data and is fast to query, but dbt rebuilds it from scratch every run. The 45-minute cost recurs daily — the opposite of the stated goal. D — ephemeral: An ephemeral model inlines as a CTE and cannot be queried or persisted on its own. Every downstream model embeds the full 500 M-row transformation as a subquery, making downstream queries extremely slow. Practical takeaway: Choose incremental when a model is expensive to rebuild in full and new data arrives in predictable, append-like batches. Always define a reliable unique_key config so dbt uses MERGE rather than blind insert, protecting against duplicates from late-arriving records.

questions.stem:
A retail data warehouse tracks a dim_customer table. A customer recently changed their
        home region from "North" to "South." The head of analytics insists that all historical
        sales reports must continue to show the region the customer was in at the time of purchase,
        not their current region.
        Which SCD type satisfies this requirement, and what structural addition does it require?
options: SCD Type 1 — overwrite the region column in place; historical queries automatically see the updated value.
SCD Type 3 — add a previous_region column alongside the current value so analysts can see both regions.
SCD Type 2 — insert a new row for the customer, setting effective_date, expiry_date, and a current-row flag, so historical facts join to the row that was active at sale time.
SCD Type 0 — retain the original region and never update it; historical integrity is preserved by default.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: SCD Type 2 is the canonical solution for preserving full history while still reflecting current state. When the customer moves from North to South, the existing row's expiry_date is set to today and is_current is set to false. A new row is inserted with the South region, a new surrogate key, today as effective_date , a far-future expiry_date , and is_current = true . Historical sales rows that carry the old surrogate key continue to join to the "North" row — so every report filters by the region that was active at sale time, exactly as the head of analytics requires. Why the distractors fail: A — Type 1: Overwrites destroy history. After the update, all historical sales would appear to belong to a "South" customer, corrupting any time-based regional analysis. B — Type 3: Adds a previous_region column, which stores exactly one prior value. This breaks the moment a customer moves a second time, and it cannot support "what was the region at an arbitrary past date?" queries. D — Type 0: Fixes the original value permanently. It works for genuinely immutable attributes (date of birth, original contract type) but not for attributes the business expects to change and track over time. Practical takeaway: SCD Type 2 is the default for history-preserving dimensions, but it comes at a cost: the fact table must carry a surrogate key (not a natural business key) so that each historical version of the customer gets its own key to join against.

questions.stem:
A Power BI report has a matrix visual with Year on rows and Region on columns.
        A measure [Total Sales] works correctly. An analyst now wants a second measure
        that always shows total sales across all regions for each year — regardless of
        which region columns are selected or which report-level filters are active.
        Which DAX expression correctly implements this?
options: CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT(dim_date, dim_date[Year]))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(dim_region))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLSELECTED(dim_region))
CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS())
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(dim_region)) removes the filter context on dim_region while leaving every other filter — including the Year row context — intact. The result is total sales across all regions for the year shown in each matrix row, exactly as required. ALL on a specific table or column surgically removes filters from that dimension only. Why the distractors fail: A — ALLEXCEPT: ALLEXCEPT(dim_date, dim_date[Year]) removes all filters except Year from the entire date table. This does not affect the Region filter, so the measure still varies by the selected region column. ALLEXCEPT is used to clear filters on one table while preserving a specific column's filter on that same table. C — ALLSELECTED: ALLSELECTED(dim_region) ignores row/column context within the visual but respects any report-level slicer or page filter on Region. The requirement says filters must be ignored regardless of what is active, so ALLSELECTED is insufficient if the user has filtered Region via a slicer. D — REMOVEFILTERS(): Called with no arguments, this removes all filters from all tables — including the Year filter from the matrix rows. Every cell in the matrix would show the grand total, not the year-specific total. Practical takeaway: The three "ALL" variants exist on a spectrum of how broadly they clear context. ALL(table/column) — targeted removal; ALLEXCEPT(table, col) — remove everything except one column; ALLSELECTED() — remove visual context but respect slicers; REMOVEFILTERS() — nuclear option. Match the scope of your filter removal to precisely the context you need to clear.

questions.stem:
A data engineering team has adopted standard Scrum: two-week sprints, story-point estimation,
        and a Definition of Done requiring that all acceptance criteria are verified before a story
        closes. Three sprints in, the team consistently fails to complete stories on time because
        data exploration mid-sprint reveals requirements that were unknowable upfront.
        Which structural characteristic of data engineering work makes this outcome predictable, even with a skilled team?
options: Data pipelines require more unit tests than software features, so velocity is lower than story-point estimates assume.
Scrum ceremonies consume too much time on large data teams, leaving insufficient sprint capacity for actual pipeline work.

Data work is inherently exploratory — the full scope and edge cases of a pipeline only become visible once you have examined the source data, making upfront sprint commitments structurally unreliable.
Story-point estimation is only reliable for UI features; data engineers should use hours-based estimation to get accurate forecasts.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: The fundamental problem is that data engineering work is exploratory in a way that software feature work is not. A software engineer asked to build a login page knows the inputs, outputs, and edge cases before they start. A data engineer asked to ingest a source system discovers missing fields, unexpected nulls, encoding problems, and business-rule inconsistencies only after connecting to the actual data. This means that scope expands mid-sprint for structural reasons, not because of poor planning or weak engineering. Scrum's two-week sprint commitment model assumes knowable, bounded scope — an assumption the data domain violates repeatedly. Why the distractors fail: A: Test volume is a process choice, not a structural property of the domain. Teams can choose not to test extensively, and the exploration problem exists regardless of test strategy. B: Ceremony overhead is a real cost but it is a process tuning problem solvable by removing ceremonies. The exploration problem cannot be removed; it is intrinsic to the work. D: Switching estimation units from points to hours changes the scale, not the fundamental unpredictability. The unknown edge cases remain unknown regardless of how you count effort. Practical takeaway: Teams that adapt Scrum for data either widen sprint buffers explicitly, use Kanban's flow-based model where work is pulled as capacity allows, or adopt Shape Up's appetite framing — "we will spend two weeks on this, and descope rather than slip." All three adaptations share a common thread: they stop treating data exploration as predictable scope.

questions.stem:
A head of analytics tells the data team: "We need to know whether our promotional campaigns
        are actually driving incremental revenue, or if we're just paying for sales that would have
        happened anyway." The team has six weeks.
        Using the "working backwards from the decision" technique, which MVP scope best fits this scenario?
options: Build a full campaign analytics platform covering all 14 campaign types, with drill-through by region, channel, customer segment, and promotion tier.
Ingest all available marketing and sales source systems into the data warehouse so the team has comprehensive data before scoping analysis.
Identify the single highest-spend campaign type, pull pre- and post-campaign sales for matched customer cohorts, and produce a one-page comparison showing incremental uplift or its absence.
Deliver a self-service dashboard where stakeholders can slice campaign data themselves, reducing future ad-hoc requests to the data team.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: Working backwards from the decision means asking: "What is the smallest dataset and analysis that would let an executive decide whether to continue, cut, or redesign this campaign type?" The decision is binary — campaigns work or they don't — so the MVP needs only enough data to answer it for the most important case. Focusing on one campaign type, using matched cohorts (a recognised proxy for incrementality), and delivering a single comparison in six weeks gives the head of analytics an actionable answer without scope creep. Why the distractors fail: A: 14 campaign types, four dimensions, and drill-through is a full product build, not a six-week MVP. This is the "perfect data warehouse" anti-pattern — solving for everything before delivering anything. B: "Ingest everything first" is the classic data pipeline anti-pattern. Full ingestion defers the analytical work until after the deadline. The decision the head of analytics needs to make does not require all 14 campaign types to be loaded. D: A self-serve dashboard is an infrastructure deliverable, not a decision deliverable. It answers no question on its own and shifts the analysis burden back to the stakeholder — the opposite of what was asked. Practical takeaway: A data MVP is judged by whether it enables a specific business decision, not by its technical completeness. If the stakeholder can make the decision with the output, the MVP succeeded — even if the underlying pipeline only covers 1 of 14 campaign types.

questions.stem:
A data engineer needs to present findings about pipeline performance to the CFO.
        She drafts a slide deck in this order: (1) how the pipeline was built, (2) the monitoring
        methodology used, (3) the metrics collected over 90 days, (4) the anomalies found,
        and finally (5) the recommendation to invest in observability tooling.
        A senior colleague tells her this structure will not land effectively with a CFO.
        What is the core communication principle the colleague is invoking?
options: The "So What" framework — each slide should end with an explicit "so what" statement so the CFO knows why each piece of data matters.
The Pyramid Principle — lead with the recommendation and its business implication, then provide supporting evidence for those who want the detail.
Audience matching — a CFO needs financial framing, so all metrics should be translated into cost-per-incident figures before the deck is presented.
The Translator Role — the engineer should convert all technical terms into plain English so the CFO can follow the methodology slides without a data background.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: The Pyramid Principle, developed by Barbara Minto, states that communication to senior audiences should start with the conclusion, then work downward through supporting arguments, then evidence. The engineer's deck follows exactly the opposite order — methodology first, recommendation last. By the time the CFO reaches slide 5, their attention has already been consumed by process details that don't speak to their priorities. The right structure: "We need to invest in observability tooling — here is why it will reduce risk and cost — here is the evidence." The methodology becomes an appendix for those who ask for it. Why the distractors fail: A — "So What" framework: This is a useful micro-level technique for ensuring each data point is connected to a business implication. It doesn't address the structural problem: even if every slide has a "so what," presenting methodology before the recommendation still forces the CFO to absorb context before they know why they're being shown anything. C — Audience matching: Translating metrics into financial language is a valid improvement, but it is a secondary concern. The structural ordering problem — recommendation last — would still exist after the translation. D — Translator Role: Plain-English translation addresses comprehension, not structure. The CFO may understand every slide and still leave without a clear ask, because the recommendation was buried at the end. Practical takeaway: With any executive audience, ask yourself: "If they walk out after slide 2, do they know the recommendation?" If the answer is no, restructure until it is yes.

questions.stem:
A data engineer asks an LLM-powered assistant to summarise a long internal report.
        The assistant returns a confident paragraph that attributes a statistic to a named source —
        but the engineer checks and that statistic does not appear anywhere in the document.
        Which property of LLM text generation makes this outcome structurally inevitable, even when the model has no explicit intent to deceive?
options: LLMs are trained on internet text that contains factual errors, so the model memorises incorrect facts and reproduces them.
The model's context window is too small to hold the entire document, so it fabricates content to fill gaps in its reading.

LLMs generate text by predicting the most plausible next token — a process optimised for fluency and coherence, not factual accuracy — so confident-sounding fabrication is an inherent output of the generation mechanism itself.
The model's temperature was set too high, causing it to sample from lower-probability tokens and produce less accurate content.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: An LLM does not "know" facts in the way a database stores them. It generates text by repeatedly predicting the most statistically plausible next token given everything that preceded it. This mechanism produces fluent, coherent prose because that is what training rewards — but it has no ground-truth verification step. When the model generates a statistic that sounds plausible in context, it does so because statistical patterns in training data associated that kind of number with that kind of statement. The resulting text is confident because confident-sounding prose is fluent prose. Hallucination is therefore a structural property of autoregressive generation, not a bug that can be patched away. Why the distractors fail: A: Training data quality matters, but even a model trained on perfectly accurate text would hallucinate. The generation mechanism — not the training data — is the root cause. A model can hallucinate a statistic that appears in no training document at all. B: Context window limits are a real constraint, but they produce truncation or refusal — not fabrication. A model that cannot fit the document will typically say so or process it in chunks, not invent content. D: Temperature affects the diversity of token sampling. High temperature increases randomness; low temperature (near zero) makes the model more deterministic. But even at temperature = 0, the model generates the single most probable continuation — which can still be factually incorrect. Temperature controls creativity, not accuracy. Practical takeaway: For production data engineering tasks, set temperature near zero to reduce variation, but treat any LLM output that references specific facts as requiring independent verification. Use RAG or citation-enforcing prompts to ground the model in a known document set.

questions.stem:
A team is building a RAG system over a large collection of internal policy documents.
        They test two chunk sizes: 200 tokens and 1,500 tokens per chunk. With 200-token chunks,
        retrieval precision is high — the right passage is almost always retrieved — but the model
        frequently gives incomplete answers. With 1,500-token chunks, answers are more complete
        but retrieval often returns an entire section when only a paragraph is relevant.
        Which statement accurately describes this tradeoff?
options: Smaller chunks are always better because they produce more precise embeddings; the incomplete answers signal a generation problem, not a chunking problem.

Chunk size controls the precision-completeness tradeoff: smaller chunks retrieve more precisely but may lack the surrounding context the model needs to answer fully; larger chunks include more context but dilute the embedding signal, reducing retrieval accuracy.
The correct fix is to use larger chunks exclusively — retrieval precision can always be recovered by increasing the number of chunks retrieved (top-k).
Chunk size has no direct effect on retrieval quality; the issue is that the embedding model needs to be replaced with a domain-specific fine-tuned version.
questions.explanation: Why B is correct: Chunk size sits at the heart of the precision-completeness tradeoff in RAG. A 200-token chunk produces an embedding that captures a very specific semantic signal — the vector closely represents that exact passage. Retrieval is precise, but if the answer to a question spans two consecutive passages, the model only sees one chunk and produces an incomplete answer. A 1,500-token chunk captures broader context and provides the model with enough material to answer multi-faceted questions, but its embedding vector must represent 1,500 tokens of mixed content — making it a noisier, more diluted signal that retrieves less precisely. Why the distractors fail: A: "Smaller is always better" is a false absolute. The incomplete answers are a direct consequence of the chunking strategy — if the answer requires two adjacent passages and only one is retrieved, no amount of generation tuning can produce a complete answer from partial context. C: Increasing top-k with large chunks retrieves more text, but it also floods the context window with loosely relevant content. This reduces the signal-to-noise ratio and can cause the model to lose focus, hallucinate, or contradict itself across the retrieved segments. D: Embedding model quality matters, but a generic embedding model on well-sized chunks outperforms a fine-tuned model on poorly-sized chunks. Chunk size is the primary lever for retrieval quality tuning. Practical takeaway: The standard production approach is to test multiple chunk sizes against a representative question set, then choose the size that maximises answer quality — often with overlap between chunks (sliding window) to preserve cross-boundary context at smaller chunk sizes.

questions.stem:
A team has implemented a pipeline where an LLM analyses incoming order data for anomalies,
        flags suspicious records, and emails the data quality team with a summary. A separate
        deterministic validation step still runs first and rejects records that fail schema checks.
        How should this architecture be classified on the AI-augmented vs. AI-autonomous spectrum?
options: AI-augmented — the LLM assists a deterministic pipeline by providing an analytical layer, but the pipeline's core logic (ingestion, schema validation, routing) is deterministic and the LLM cannot alter execution decisions or pipeline structure.
AI-autonomous — because the LLM generates content (the summary email) that triggers a human action, it is driving the outcome of the pipeline process.
AI-autonomous — the LLM is making decisions about which records are suspicious, which is an execution decision that a deterministic rule would otherwise make.
Neither — a pipeline that includes both deterministic and AI steps is a hybrid architecture and cannot be placed on the augmented/autonomous spectrum.
questions.explanation: Why A is correct: The AI-augmented vs. AI-autonomous distinction is about who (or what) drives execution decisions in the pipeline. In this architecture, the pipeline runs deterministically: records arrive, schema validation runs, valid records proceed to the LLM anomaly layer, and the LLM produces a human-readable summary. The LLM's output is advisory — it flags records and emails a team, but it does not decide whether to continue ingestion, reroute data, trigger a remediation job, or alter the pipeline's execution graph. A human reviews the email and decides what to do. The pipeline would complete in exactly the same structural way regardless of whether the LLM found anomalies or not. Why the distractors fail: B: Generating an email that prompts a human action is advisory output, not autonomous decision-making. The human is still in the loop before any consequential action is taken. Producing a summary report does not give the LLM control over pipeline execution. C: Flagging suspicious records is pattern recognition, not an execution decision. The records are not rerouted, quarantined, or deleted by the LLM — the flagging is advisory. If the LLM were triggering a hold, rerouting to a quarantine table, or halting ingestion, that would shift toward autonomous territory. D: The spectrum is specifically designed to classify mixed architectures. Most real production systems are somewhere on the spectrum between fully deterministic and fully autonomous — the question is how much of the execution logic is driven by AI decisions. Practical takeaway: A useful test: "If the LLM returned garbage output, would the pipeline still complete correctly?" If yes, it is augmented. If no — because the AI output drives routing, triggers downstream jobs, or modifies pipeline structure — it is moving toward autonomous. Design autonomy levels deliberately and document which decisions have human oversight.

questions.stem:
A supply chain team wants to automatically process incoming supplier emails: extracting the
        supplier name, invoice number, and delivery date from each email body, then routing the
        email to the correct operations sub-team based on its content type (invoice, delay notice,
        or query).
        Which pair of NLP task types is needed to implement this solution?
options: Summarisation and sentiment analysis — summarise each email and assess whether the supplier's tone is positive or negative to determine urgency.
Text generation and summarisation — generate a structured summary of each email, then use that summary to decide the routing destination.

Named Entity Recognition (NER) and text classification — NER extracts the structured fields (supplier, invoice number, date) and classification assigns each email to the correct content-type category.
Conversational AI and text classification — a conversational AI asks follow-up questions to clarify ambiguous emails before classification routes them.
questions.explanation: Why C is correct: The scenario has two distinct requirements: extracting structured fields and categorising the email. These map precisely to two NLP task types. NER (Named Entity Recognition) identifies and extracts named entities — specific spans of text that refer to people, organisations, dates, IDs, and other typed values. "Invoice #INV-4482", "Acme Supplies Ltd", and "15 July 2026" are exactly the kinds of entities NER is designed to locate and label. The output is structured: a dictionary of field names and extracted values. Text classification assigns the full email to a predefined category. With sufficient training examples ("invoice", "delay notice", "query"), a classifier learns the linguistic patterns that distinguish each type and routes new emails accordingly. Classification output is a label (and confidence score), not extracted content. Why the distractors fail: A — Summarisation + sentiment: Summarisation condenses content without extracting structured fields. Sentiment analysis measures positive/negative/neutral tone — irrelevant for routing based on content type rather than emotional valence. B — Text generation + summarisation: Both tasks produce free text, not structured output. Routing requires a categorical decision, not a generated paragraph. Extracting invoice numbers from generated summaries introduces an unnecessary hallucination risk. D — Conversational AI + classification: Adding a conversational loop for ambiguous emails is a valid human-in-the-loop enhancement, but it is not necessary for the core requirement — and conversational AI cannot replace NER for field extraction in high-volume automated pipelines. Practical takeaway: Map each requirement separately: "extract specific typed values from text" → NER; "assign text to one of N predefined categories" → classification. Combining two narrow, well-scoped models almost always outperforms one large generative model for high-volume, structured extraction tasks.

questions.stem: A team is in sprint planning. The product owner proposes the story: "As a user, I want the entire data platform migrated to the cloud." A senior PM says this story violates the INVEST criteria and cannot enter the sprint. Which violation is MOST fundamental for sprint planning purposes?
options: Independent — the story depends on infrastructure owned by the network team, creating a cross-team dependency that blocks completion.
Negotiable — the word ‘entire’ eliminates any room to negotiate scope with engineering during the sprint.
Small — the story is far too large to complete within a single sprint and cannot be meaningfully estimated or committed to.
Testable — no single pass/fail acceptance criterion can be written for a migration of this scope.
questions.explanation: INVEST stands for Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable. For sprint planning, the Small criterion is the gating constraint: if a story cannot be completed within one sprint, it does not belong in the sprint backlog at all — it belongs in the epic or theme layer and must be decomposed. All other INVEST violations become moot if the story is unsized for a sprint. In practice, a story this large is an Epic (or even a Theme), not a User Story. The PM’s job is to decompose it into sprint-sized stories — each independently valuable and testable — before any planning conversation begins. The other options identify real violations but are secondary: you cannot negotiate, test, or check dependencies on a story that cannot fit in a sprint in the first place. Practical takeaway: Before any sprint planning, apply the Size check first. If the answer is ‘no one knows how long this takes,’ decompose before estimating.

questions.stem: True or False: The user story “As a data analyst, I want a dashboard showing daily sales by region” is complete as written, because it follows the standard As a… I want… format and clearly identifies the user, the feature, and the deliverable.
questions.explanation: FALSE. The story is missing the “So that…” clause, which states the business goal or benefit the user is trying to achieve. Without this clause the team does not know WHY the feature is needed, which means they cannot make intelligent design tradeoffs. Should the dashboard refresh hourly or daily? Should it include YoY comparisons? Can it be a simple table or must it be a visual chart? All of these decisions depend on understanding what the analyst is trying to accomplish. A story without a goal also tends to fail the Testable criterion — you cannot write meaningful acceptance criteria if you don’t know what success looks like for the user. Complete form: “As a data analyst, I want a dashboard showing daily sales by region so that I can identify underperforming regions before the weekly business review and prepare targeted questions.” That ‘so that’ clause changes every implementation decision.

questions.stem: A tech lead says a proposed story “isn’t ready” for sprint planning. The product owner asks what that means. Which of the following best describes a Definition of Ready (DoR) in an agile data team context?
options: The engineering team has accepted the estimate, moved the card to ‘In Progress,’ and assigned it to a developer.
The story satisfies pre-agreed entry criteria — such as having clear acceptance criteria, a scoped data source, and no unresolved blockers — before it is allowed into a sprint.
The story has passed code review, automated tests, and is ready to be deployed to the production environment.
The product owner has written the story in the correct As a… I want… So that… format and added it to the backlog.
questions.explanation: The Definition of Ready (DoR) is the team’s agreed checklist of conditions a story must meet before it enters a sprint. Common DoR items for a data story include: acceptance criteria are written and agreed, the source data has been profiled and its quirks are documented, dependencies are identified and unblocked, and the story is small enough to complete within the sprint. The DoR exists because pulling under-specified stories into a sprint is the single most common cause of mid-sprint scope expansion in data work — the team discovers the actual requirements only after starting. This is distinct from the Definition of Done (DoD), which defines what ‘complete’ means after the work is finished (tests pass, pipeline is monitored, stakeholder has signed off). Practical takeaway: A healthy DoR for data stories always includes ‘source data profiled’ — the most common hidden surprise that blows sprint commitments.

questions.stem: A director of product asks the data team to present their 12-month roadmap. The team shares a Gantt chart with exact start and end dates for 14 projects. The director says this artifact creates false precision and sets the wrong expectations. Which roadmap format is more appropriate for communicating strategic direction without committing to specific dates?
options: A release-based roadmap — grouping work into named releases (v1.0, v2.0) gives stakeholders milestones to communicate without committing to calendar dates.
A Now / Next / Later roadmap — it communicates relative priority and strategic direction without date commitments that are almost certainly wrong 6–12 months out.
A flat ranked backlog — a numbered priority list gives stakeholders the clearest signal of what gets built first.
An OKR board — surfacing the Objectives and Key Results the team is targeting replaces any need for a project-level roadmap.
questions.explanation: The Now / Next / Later (or Now / Next / Future) roadmap communicates three things clearly: what the team is actively building, what is prioritised and coming soon, and what is on the horizon but not yet scheduled. Critically, it contains no dates — which removes the false precision problem entirely. Stakeholders understand direction and relative priority without internalising specific delivery dates that will change as work is discovered. A Gantt chart 12 months out implies a level of certainty that does not exist in any data product or software project; it trains stakeholders to hold the team to dates rather than outcomes. Release-based roadmaps still imply sequence and scope commitments. A flat backlog lacks strategic narrative. OKRs complement roadmaps but cannot replace them. Practical takeaway: Use Now/Next/Later for external stakeholder communication; add estimated quarters only when the roadmap is mature and the work is well-understood.

questions.stem: True or False: When a business stakeholder presents a vague request like “we need better reporting,” the correct PM response is to immediately write it as a user story and add it to the sprint backlog so the team can begin estimating it.
questions.explanation: FALSE. “We need better reporting” is a Theme at best — a broad area of investment, not a buildable unit of work. Writing it directly as a story skips the discovery work that makes stories actionable. The PM should first run structured discovery: who is frustrated, with which reports, in what workflow, and what decision do they make differently with better data? That discovery converts the theme into Epics (e.g., “Sales team visibility into pipeline conversion”), and Epics decompose into sprint-sized Stories (e.g., “As a regional sales manager, I want a daily deal-stage conversion funnel so that I can identify where deals stall before my Monday review”). Pulling an unresearched theme into the backlog produces stories that fail INVEST on at least three criteria — they are not Independent, not Estimable, and not Testable. The result is invariably mid-sprint scope expansion and rework. Practical takeaway: Discovery is not optional overhead; it is the work that makes all subsequent sprint work efficient.

questions.stem: A fraud detection model is trained on a dataset where 98% of transactions are legitimate and 2% are fraudulent. After training, the model achieves 98.1% accuracy on the test set. Which statement best describes whether this result indicates good performance?
options: 98.1% accuracy is strong — the model correctly classifies nearly all transactions and is production-ready.

Accuracy is misleading here — a model that predicts ‘legitimate’ for every single transaction would also achieve 98% accuracy, so this result tells us almost nothing about the model’s ability to detect fraud.
The model is under-performing — on a balanced dataset a 98.1%-accurate fraud model would be considered production-grade, and class imbalance is easily fixed by oversampling.
The result is acceptable for a first version; precision and recall should be added to the monitoring dashboard after deployment.
questions.explanation: This is the canonical illustration of why accuracy fails on imbalanced classes. A trivial classifier that always outputs ‘legitimate’ achieves 98% accuracy — it never detects a single fraud but scores nearly identically to the trained model. The 0.1% improvement over the trivial baseline could plausibly come from a handful of easy negatives, not from any genuine fraud-detection ability. The correct metrics here are precision (of the transactions flagged as fraud, how many actually were?) and recall (of all actual frauds, how many did the model catch?). In fraud detection, recall is typically the priority metric because missing a fraud is more costly than a false alarm. Practical takeaway: Always check class distribution before choosing a primary evaluation metric. Whenever one class represents less than 10–15% of the data, treat accuracy as uninformative and lead with precision, recall, F1, or AUC-PR.

questions.stem: True or False: For a medical screening model that flags patients for a potentially fatal disease, it is more important to maximise precision (avoid false positives) than to maximise recall (avoid false negatives), because unnecessary follow-up tests waste hospital resources.
questions.explanation: FALSE. In a life-threatening medical screening context, a false negative — missing a patient who actually has the disease — is catastrophically more costly than a false positive, which results in an unnecessary follow-up test. A patient with an undetected fatal disease goes untreated; a patient incorrectly flagged gets an extra test. The relative cost of these two error types overwhelmingly favours maximising recall (sensitivity) and accepting lower precision. Precision becomes the priority metric when false positives carry high costs. Classic examples: a spam filter (a legitimate email going to spam costs the user something real), a credit fraud block (blocking a legitimate transaction immediately harms the customer), or a content moderation system where over-removal damages user trust. The key principle is to identify which type of error is more costly for the specific business context, then choose the metric that penalises that error most directly. Practical takeaway: Never default to a single metric — always articulate which error type is more costly before choosing your optimisation target.

questions.stem: A binary classifier for detecting defective products on a manufacturing line has precision 0.95 and recall 0.40. A colleague proposes reporting the F1 score to leadership as the single summary metric. Which response is most accurate?
options: Report F1 — it is the standard single metric for all binary classification and always preferred over reporting precision and recall separately.
Report F1 — precision of 0.95 and recall of 0.40 average to a respectable score that would reassure leadership.

Be cautious about F1 here — with precision 0.95 and recall 0.40, the F1 score (approximately 0.56) masks a serious recall failure; leadership decisions depend on knowing that 60% of defects are being missed.
Neither metric is appropriate — on manufacturing defect data, accuracy is the only valid metric because production lines are assumed to produce balanced class distributions.
questions.explanation: F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall: 2 × (0.95 × 0.40) / (0.95 + 0.40) ≈ 0.565. Reporting 0.565 to leadership buries the critical signal that 60% of defects are escaping to customers undetected. In manufacturing, an undetected defect that reaches a customer can trigger recalls, safety incidents, and reputational damage — consequences that dwarf the cost of over-inspecting. F1 is most useful when precision and recall are reasonably balanced. When they diverge sharply — especially when one reflects a catastrophic failure mode — reporting them separately, with a plain-language explanation of what the worse one means operationally, is the responsible choice. The arithmetic mean (0.675) would be even more misleading; the harmonic mean at least penalises the gap. Practical takeaway: Always ask ‘which error is the kill shot for this business’ before choosing a single summary metric. If the answer is recall failure, lead with recall in the stakeholder presentation.

questions.stem: A data science team builds a model to detect rare equipment failures (0.5% of sensor events are actual failures). They report AUC-ROC = 0.92 and conclude the model is excellent. A senior reviewer says this conclusion is incomplete. What is the most likely concern?
options: AUC-ROC cannot be computed on imbalanced datasets; the team should have used balanced accuracy instead.

AUC-ROC can be misleadingly high on imbalanced data because the large number of true negatives inflates the metric; Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) is a more informative complement on rare-event problems.
A 0.92 AUC signals overfitting — production models for rare events should target AUC between 0.70 and 0.80 to demonstrate generalisability.
The concern is the train-test split; with a 0.5% positive rate, random splitting will produce a test set with almost no positives, making AUC numerically unstable.
questions.explanation: ROC-AUC measures how well the model separates positives from negatives across all classification thresholds. On a dataset with 0.5% failures, 99.5% of events are true negatives. A model that classifies negatives very well can accumulate a high true-negative rate across the ROC curve, producing a strong AUC — even if its precision at the operating threshold is terrible. The Precision-Recall curve, by contrast, focuses entirely on the positive class and is far more sensitive to performance on the minority class. PR-AUC near 1.0 on a rare-event problem indicates the model is genuinely good at catching failures; PR-AUC near the base rate (0.005 in this case) indicates it is not. Best practice for rare-event classification: always report both ROC-AUC and PR-AUC, and set alert thresholds using PR-curve analysis rather than ROC. Practical takeaway: When someone reports only AUC-ROC on a dataset with less than 5% positives, ask to see the PR curve before accepting the conclusion.
