questions.stem: True or False: For a demand forecasting model where occasional extreme over-forecasts carry severe supply chain consequences such as excess inventory and warehouse overflow, RMSE is a more appropriate primary evaluation metric than MAE.
questions.explanation: TRUE. RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) squares the error before averaging, which means large errors contribute disproportionately to the final score. This is exactly the property you want when large errors are disproportionately costly. A single 10,000-unit over-forecast that causes warehouse overflow is not just ‘one big error’ — it may trigger emergency storage fees, spoilage, or capital lockup that costs orders of magnitude more than 100 smaller errors of 100 units each. RMSE’s squaring reflects that economic reality. MAE treats all errors on a linear scale — a 10,000-unit error costs exactly 100 times a 100-unit error under MAE, which understates the actual operational damage. Use MAE when errors are roughly equally costly regardless of magnitude, or when you want a metric that is robust to outliers and easy to interpret in original units. Use RMSE when large errors are genuinely more damaging than their linear scale implies. Practical takeaway: Always connect your choice of metric to the cost function of the business, not just mathematical convenience.

questions.stem: An e-commerce team runs an A/B test on a new checkout flow and gets a p-value of 0.03. A product manager announces: “there is a 97% probability the new checkout is better.” Which statement correctly identifies the error in this interpretation?
options: The PM is correct — p = 0.03 means a 97% probability the result is not due to chance.

The PM is wrong — a p-value of 0.03 means: if the null hypothesis were true (no real difference), there is only a 3% probability of observing a result this extreme or more extreme by chance. It says nothing about the probability that the new checkout is better.
The PM is wrong — p = 0.03 means the result is 97% reproducible; if the test were repeated, it would succeed 97% of the time.
The p-value interpretation is correct, but the PM must also verify the confidence interval does not cross zero before concluding significance.
questions.explanation: The p-value is one of the most systematically misunderstood statistics in business. It answers a very specific conditional question: “Assuming the null hypothesis is true (there is no difference), how probable is it to observe data this extreme or more extreme?” It does NOT answer: “How likely is it that our variant is better?” or “How likely is it that our result will replicate?” Interpreting p = 0.03 as ‘97% confidence the variant is superior’ conflates the probability of data given the null with the probability of the null given the data — a logical inversion known as the prosecutor’s fallacy. To directly estimate P(variant B > variant A), a Bayesian framework is needed. Practical takeaway: When briefing executives on A/B test results, translate p-values into plain language about what question was actually answered, and be explicit about what the number does not mean. A 95% confidence interval on the lift estimate is usually more intuitive and business-relevant than a p-value alone.

questions.stem: True or False: It is valid practice to monitor an A/B test daily and stop it as soon as you observe p < 0.05 for the first time, because the test has reached statistical significance and continuing it would not change the conclusion.
questions.explanation: FALSE. This is the ‘peeking problem’ — one of the most common errors that invalidates A/B tests in practice. Each time you check results and compare against a significance threshold, you are effectively running an additional hypothesis test. If you check daily for 14 days and stop at the first p < 0.05, your actual Type I error rate (false positive rate) inflates far above 5% — simulations show it can reach 30% or more over 20 peeks. The experiment must be pre-registered: fix the sample size (or duration), the significance threshold, and the primary metric before any data is collected. The analysis should be run exactly once, at the pre-registered stopping point. Sequential testing frameworks — such as the Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) or always-valid inference methods — do allow valid early stopping, but they require different statistical machinery than a standard fixed-horizon t-test. Practical takeaway: Lock the stopping rule before you start, not after you see a number you like.

questions.stem: A data team runs an A/B test for a new recommendation algorithm, concludes it has no statistically significant effect, and recommends not shipping it. Three months later, a separate analysis reveals the algorithm actually improved click-through rate by 8%. Which type of error did the original test commit?
options: Type I error (false positive) — the team incorrectly rejected the null hypothesis and concluded there was an effect when there was none.
Type II error (false negative) — the team failed to detect a real effect, concluding ‘no significant difference’ when the algorithm genuinely outperformed the control.
Neither — failing to reject the null hypothesis is the correct statistical conclusion whenever the data is insufficient; no formal error was committed.
A sampling bias error — the Type I / Type II framework only applies when the null is rejected; this outcome falls outside it.
questions.explanation: A Type II error (false negative) occurs when a test fails to reject a null hypothesis that is actually false — a real effect exists but the test did not detect it. Here, the algorithm genuinely improved click-through by 8% (the null was false), but the test concluded ‘no significant difference’ (failed to reject the null). The probability of committing a Type II error is (1 − statistical power). Low power is the most common cause: too small a sample size, too short a test duration, or too high a significance threshold. The business cost of a Type II error in this scenario was significant: a genuine 8% CTR improvement sat unused for three months. Practical takeaway: Before running a test, calculate the required sample size to achieve 80% or 90% power for the minimum effect size you care about. An underpowered test is not a neutral ‘no result’ — it is a poorly designed experiment that systematically suppresses real effects.

questions.stem: An A/B test on a new landing page runs for 6 weeks with 2 million visitors per group. The new page increases conversion rate from 3.000% to 3.002%, with p < 0.001. The team debates whether to ship it. Which principle should govern this decision?
options: Ship immediately — p < 0.001 is extremely strong statistical evidence; any measured improvement justifies implementation.

The result is statistically significant but may not be business significant — a 0.002 percentage point lift on 2 million visitors is roughly 40 extra conversions per week; whether that justifies engineering and maintenance cost is a business decision, not a statistical one.
Re-run the test with a smaller sample size to get a less biased effect size estimate before deciding.
Reject the result — with samples this large, virtually any test will reach significance regardless of the true effect; a Bonferroni-corrected threshold should be used.
questions.explanation: With very large samples, even trivially small effects become statistically significant. Statistical significance only tells you the effect is real and non-zero — it says nothing about whether the effect is large enough to matter. A 0.002 percentage point conversion lift generating approximately 40 extra conversions per week must be evaluated against its costs: engineering time to ship, ongoing maintenance burden, increased code complexity, potential page-load impact, and the opportunity cost of not building something else. That is a business calculation, not a statistical one. The correct principle is business significance: is the effect large enough to justify action? Practical takeaway: Always pre-register a Minimum Detectable Effect (MDE) that represents the smallest lift worth shipping. If the observed lift is below the MDE, the test answered a different question than the one you care about, and the decision defaults to ‘don’t ship’ on business grounds regardless of the p-value.

questions.stem: After an A/B test completes, a product manager asks: “What is the probability that variant B is actually better than variant A?” The data scientist has run a standard frequentist t-test and obtained p = 0.04. Which response is most accurate?
options: “There is a 96% probability that B is better than A, since 1 − p = 0.96.”

“The frequentist framework cannot directly answer that question — it can only tell us the probability of observing this data if there were no difference. A Bayesian A/B test would directly compute P(B > A) and express it as a probability like 94%.”
“p = 0.04 means B has a 4% chance of being worse than A, so there is a 96% probability B is better.”
“The frequentist confidence interval answers this question: if the 95% CI on the lift excludes zero, then P(B > A) = 1 − alpha = 0.95.”
questions.explanation: This question exposes the core philosophical gap between frequentist and Bayesian inference. The frequentist framework treats the true effect as a fixed (unknown) constant and treats the data as random. Its output — the p-value — is a statement about data probability given a hypothesis: P(data | H₀). It cannot directly produce a statement about hypothesis probability given data: P(H | data). The PM’s question is inherently a Bayesian one. A Bayesian A/B test places a prior on the conversion rates, updates it with observed data, and produces a posterior distribution. From that posterior you can directly read P(B > A) — a natural probability statement the PM can act on. Options A and C both make the same error: they invert the conditional probability. Option D confuses the confidence level (which is a property of the procedure, not a probability about the true effect) with a posterior probability. Practical takeaway: When stakeholders want a probability that a variant is better, a Bayesian framework communicates more naturally than p-values. Many modern experimentation platforms (Optimizely, VWO, Google Optimize) now offer Bayesian reporting for exactly this reason.

questions.stem: A retailer’s point-of-sale data shows steady weekly demand of 100 units. The retailer orders 120 units per week from its distributor as a safety buffer. The distributor, seeing 120-unit orders, orders 150 from the manufacturer. The manufacturer now plans production for 180 units per week. Which supply chain phenomenon does this illustrate?
options: Lead time compression — each upstream party reduces perceived lead times, artificially inflating planned order quantities.
The bullwhip effect — small fluctuations in end-consumer demand are amplified at each upstream stage, causing increasingly volatile order patterns the further you move from the actual consumer.
Demand sensing failure — the retailer’s POS system is not sharing real-time sell-through data with upstream partners, causing each party to independently buffer.
Safety stock miscalculation — each party applies the same safety stock formula to progressively larger order quantities, compounding the error multiplicatively.
questions.explanation: The bullwhip effect (Forrester effect) describes how a small variation in consumer demand propagates upstream as increasingly amplified order swings. Even when end demand is perfectly stable at 100 units, each upstream party adds its own safety buffer based on the orders it sees — not on actual consumer sell-through. The result is the manufacturer planning for 180 units to support 100 units of real demand. This phenomenon drives excess inventory, unnecessary production cycles, emergency stockouts, and wasted capital throughout supply chains. For data engineers, the bullwhip effect is a direct motivation for demand sensing: sharing point-of-sale and inventory data in near real time with all supply chain tiers so each party plans against actual consumer demand rather than the distorted signal arriving through orders. Practical takeaway: A data platform that exposes POS velocity to manufacturers and distributors directly attacks the information asymmetry that produces the bullwhip effect.

questions.stem: True or False: Before deploying an ARIMA or ML-based forecasting model to production, a team should always establish a naive baseline forecast and verify the complex model meaningfully outperforms it — because failing to beat a naive baseline indicates the model has not learned useful signal beyond simple persistence.
questions.explanation: TRUE. The naive forecast — predicting that tomorrow’s demand equals today’s, or that this week equals last week — is the absolute minimum bar any forecasting model must clear. If an ARIMA or ML model does not outperform this baseline on hold-out data, it has not learned any structure beyond ‘the recent past repeats.’ This is a common production failure: teams invest in complex models that are operationally expensive and interpretively opaque, yet are statistically indistinguishable from — or worse than — a naive forecast. Baselines also serve a communication purpose: they provide a concrete benchmark for explaining model value to non-technical stakeholders. Saying ‘our model reduces forecast error by 30% over the naive baseline’ is far more meaningful than an abstract MAPE figure. Practical takeaway: Always report model performance relative to a named baseline. If the baseline is not documented, you cannot tell whether the model is adding value or consuming engineering resources for no gain.

questions.stem: A forecasting team evaluates three models using MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error). Models A and B produce valid MAPE scores, but Model C cannot compute a valid MAPE for one SKU category. Which scenario most likely explains why Model C’s MAPE computation fails for that category?
options: The category contains too many SKUs; MAPE degrades numerically when averaged across more than 50 product lines.

The category contains items with zero or near-zero actual demand in some periods; MAPE divides by the actual value, so a zero or near-zero denominator produces undefined or infinite percentage errors.
The category uses weekly rather than daily demand buckets; MAPE requires daily granularity to compute valid percentage errors.
Model C applies a log transform internally; log-transformed outputs cannot be directly evaluated with MAPE without inverse transformation.
questions.explanation: MAPE is defined as the mean of |actual − forecast| / |actual|. When actual demand is zero in a given period, you are dividing by zero — the result is undefined. When actual demand is near-zero (e.g., 1 unit), even a small absolute error produces a 100%+ percentage error, inflating MAPE to absurd levels. This is MAPE’s fundamental instability on intermittent or low-volume demand — the very pattern that characterises spare parts, slow-moving SKUs, and seasonal items with off-season zeros. Alternatives include SMAPE (Symmetric MAPE, which averages actual and forecast in the denominator), MASE (Mean Absolute Scaled Error, which scales errors by the naive forecast), or simply MAE in original units for products where absolute error is more meaningful than percentage error. Practical takeaway: Before choosing a forecast accuracy metric, profile the demand distributions. If a significant portion of periods have zero or very low demand, eliminate MAPE from consideration for that SKU tier.

questions.stem: A supply chain analyst compares a 12-period simple moving average against single exponential smoothing (alpha = 0.3) to forecast demand for a product that has shown a consistent upward trend for 6 months. Which statement correctly identifies a shared limitation of both methods for this scenario?
options: Neither method handles seasonality well; both should be replaced with a Holt-Winters model, which separately estimates level, trend, and seasonal components.

Both simple moving average and single exponential smoothing assume a stationary (flat) underlying level; neither has a built-in mechanism to track a persistent upward trend, causing both to systematically lag behind the actual demand.
Single exponential smoothing with alpha = 0.3 overweights old data; setting alpha to 0.9 would correctly track the trend without changing the method.
The 12-period moving average is superior for trending data because its longer window smooths out noise and produces inherently more accurate trend-following forecasts.
questions.explanation: Both simple moving average and single (simple) exponential smoothing are level-only models — they estimate the current average level of the series, but they have no explicit component for trend. On a steadily trending series, both methods produce forecasts that persistently sit below the actual observations because they are always averaging in past values that predate the trend’s current level. This systematic under-forecast is called lag bias. The solution is Holt’s double exponential smoothing (or Holt-Winters for data with both trend and seasonality), which adds a second smoothing equation specifically tracking the trend component. Increasing alpha to 0.9 makes exponential smoothing react faster to recent values, which partially reduces lag, but it does not solve the structural absence of a trend equation — it just makes the model noisier. Practical takeaway: Before choosing a forecasting method, visualise the time series. Flat with noise? Level-only methods work. Consistent trend? Add a trend component. Clear seasonality? Add a seasonal component. Match the model’s structure to the data’s structure.

questions.stem: A warehouse manager needs weekly demand forecasts for 500 spare parts. For 80% of these SKUs, demand is intermittent — many weeks show zero demand, with occasional spikes of 1–5 units. Which forecasting approach is most appropriate for these intermittent SKUs?
options: ARIMA — its differencing and autoregressive components handle irregular gaps in demand by modelling the underlying process as a correlated time series.
A deep learning model — neural networks can capture the complex non-linear patterns in intermittent demand better than any classical method, and require minimal feature engineering.

Croston’s method or the Syntetos-Boylan Approximation (SBA), which separately models the interval between demand occurrences and the demand size per occurrence, avoiding the biases that standard exponential smoothing produces on zero-inflated series.
A 4-week simple moving average — the short window ensures the forecast reacts quickly to demand spikes without accumulating too many zero-demand periods in the average.
questions.explanation: Intermittent demand (also called lumpy or sporadic demand) violates the assumptions of standard forecasting methods. ARIMA expects autocorrelated, continuous signal — long strings of zeros produce near-zero autocorrelation and make the model return forecasts close to zero, missing the occasional spike entirely. Standard exponential smoothing applied to a zero-inflated series gives biased estimates because it conflates two separate processes: how often demand occurs, and how much demand arrives when it does occur. Croston’s method separates these: one exponential smoothing equation tracks the inter-demand interval, another tracks the demand size per active period. The forecast is the ratio of the two smoothed values. SBA (Syntetos-Boylan Approximation) applies a bias correction to Croston’s estimates. A 4-week moving average dilutes the rare demand events with zeros, systematically under-forecasting. Deep learning models can work well at scale with sufficient data, but most intermittent SKUs have too few non-zero observations to train them reliably. Practical takeaway: Segment your SKU portfolio by demand pattern — smooth, lumpy/intermittent, and truly erratic — and apply a different forecasting method to each segment.

questions.stem: A data pipeline ingests customer orders from a REST API and appends records to a Bronze Delta table daily. After a 4-hour upstream API outage, the team reruns the previous 24 hours of ingestion. Analysts then discover duplicate order records propagating through Silver and Gold layers. Which Bronze-layer design requirement was violated?
options: Idempotency — a Bronze write should produce the same result whether executed once or multiple times; appending on rerun instead of overwriting introduced duplicate records.
Partitioning — Bronze tables must be partitioned by ingestion_date to prevent overlapping file writes from reruns.
Schema enforcement — Silver should reject duplicate records at the validation step before they reach Gold.
Data lineage — the pipeline lacked metadata to distinguish original records from rerun records.
questions.explanation: Idempotency is the guarantee that running a pipeline operation once or N times produces the same result. Bronze layers must be idempotent because reruns are operationally inevitable — upstream outages, late-arriving data, or retry logic can all trigger a second write for the same time window. The correct pattern is overwrite-by-partition: the pipeline writes to a specific date/time partition and uses INSERT OVERWRITE or replaceWhere so that a rerun replaces the same partition rather than appending again. Appending without deduplication creates duplicates that propagate through Silver and Gold, corrupting all downstream metrics. Partitioning alone is a performance tool, not an idempotency guarantee. Schema enforcement validates column types, not row uniqueness. Lineage tracking helps diagnose problems but does not prevent them. Practical takeaway: design every Bronze write as a bounded, overwritable partition so reruns are safe by construction, not by careful coordination.

questions.stem: True or False: A team running a Medallion Architecture on Databricks enables Delta Lake's Change Data Feed (CDF) on their Bronze table, allowing downstream Silver pipelines to consume only rows inserted or updated since the last run — rather than scanning the full Bronze table on every execution.
questions.explanation: TRUE. Delta Lake's Change Data Feed records every row-level insert, update, and delete in a metadata log attached to the table. Downstream Silver pipelines can consume this feed using the readChangeFeed option or the table_changes() function, reading only the changes since their last watermark rather than scanning the full Bronze table. As Bronze tables grow to hundreds of gigabytes, this incremental processing pattern keeps Silver pipeline runtimes roughly constant relative to daily change volume rather than total table size. CDF must be enabled at table creation time (or via ALTER TABLE SET TBLPROPERTIES) because it only records changes from the moment it is activated. It complements the Medallion Architecture's separation of concerns: Bronze still lands raw data idempotently, and Silver efficiently propagates only new changes downstream. Practical takeaway: enable CDF on Bronze tables during initial design to avoid costly retrofits later as the table grows.

questions.stem: A small analytics team has a single well-structured transactional source, five analysts querying stable daily KPIs, and limited engineering capacity. A data engineer proposes a two-tier Bronze → Gold model, skipping the Silver layer entirely. Which statement best describes when this simplification is appropriate?
options: Two tiers are never appropriate — Silver always provides essential data quality protection and skipping it creates unacceptable technical debt.
Two tiers are preferred for large organisations with many sources — fewer tiers reduce operational complexity at scale.

Two tiers are appropriate when source data is clean and well-structured, the transformation from raw to serving is direct, and there are no cross-source joins or complex cleansing requirements that a Silver layer would address.
Two tiers are only valid for real-time streaming architectures; batch pipelines always require a Silver buffer between ingestion and serving.
questions.explanation: The Silver tier addresses specific engineering problems: cross-source schema harmonisation, reusable cleansing logic shared by multiple Gold consumers, deduplication, and type standardisation across inconsistent upstream systems. When none of these problems exist — a single clean source, a stable well-typed schema, and a direct one-to-one transformation to serving — Silver adds pipeline overhead without adding value. The right time to add Silver is when you can articulate what problem it solves: if multiple Gold tables would each independently re-implement the same cleansing logic, that shared logic belongs in Silver. Two-tier models are pragmatic for early-stage products, prototypes, and single-source analytical workflows. The claim that Silver is mandatory regardless of source count misunderstands the principle — Silver's value scales with source diversity and consumer count, not organisation size or execution mode. Practical takeaway: add tiers when you feel their absence, not by architectural convention.

questions.stem: A data engineering team plans to use dbt for all Medallion Architecture transformations across Bronze, Silver, and Gold. A senior engineer objects to using dbt for Bronze ingestion. Which argument best supports keeping Bronze ingestion outside of dbt?
options: dbt should own all three tiers — it provides version control, testing, and lineage for every transformation and works equally well for all layers.
dbt should exclusively own the Gold tier; Silver and Bronze are better managed with Python scripts to reduce SQL execution overhead on large raw datasets.
dbt cannot be used in a Medallion Architecture because its models are always recomputed from scratch and cannot support Bronze's incremental append pattern.

dbt is a transformation tool whose core abstraction is a SELECT statement producing a derived table; Bronze is a landing zone that must receive source data exactly as it arrived with no transformation — which is the purpose of a dedicated ingestion tool like ADF, Fivetran, or Airbyte.
questions.explanation: dbt's fundamental abstraction is a SQL SELECT statement that reads from existing tables and produces a derived table or view. Bronze is a landing zone: its job is to receive source data exactly as it arrived, with no transformation, no business logic, and no schema alteration. Running Bronze ingestion through dbt requires writing SELECT logic that copies source data verbatim — a misuse of dbt's design purpose that also defeats Bronze's raw-copy principle. Dedicated ingestion tools (ADF, Fivetran, Airbyte) are purpose-built for source connectivity, incremental change detection, authentication handling, and raw data capture. dbt's incremental models can technically write Bronze-style append tables, but this conflates ingestion and transformation concerns that are better kept separate for reliability and maintainability. The clear separation is: ingestion tools produce Bronze, dbt transforms Bronze→Silver and Silver→Gold. Practical takeaway: assign tools to their designed responsibilities — ingestion tools ingest, transformation tools transform.

questions.stem: True or False: Event streaming architectures using tools like Kafka and Spark Structured Streaming cannot implement a Medallion Architecture because the Bronze → Silver → Gold tier structure requires discrete batch checkpoints between layers to enforce data quality.
questions.explanation: FALSE. The Medallion Architecture defines data quality contracts across tiers — Bronze holds raw data, Silver holds cleansed data, Gold holds curated domain models — and this tier structure is fully compatible with streaming execution. In a streaming Lakehouse pattern, Kafka topics or Event Hubs serve as the Bronze landing zone. Spark Structured Streaming jobs continuously read from these sources, apply Silver-layer quality checks in micro-batches, and write to Silver Delta tables. Separate streaming or scheduled batch jobs apply Gold-layer aggregations and domain logic. Each tier still enforces its quality contract; the data flows continuously rather than in daily batches. Databricks Delta Live Tables explicitly supports streaming Medallion pipelines as a first-class pattern. The choice between batch and streaming should be driven by the latency requirement — sub-hour freshness demands streaming; overnight analytics can use batch. The Medallion tier structure works with either execution model. Practical takeaway: adopt streaming when latency requires it; the Medallion architecture accommodates both.

questions.stem: A faulty deployment writes 3 hours of corrupted records to a Delta table storing retail transactions. The team needs to restore the table to its last verified-good state before the deployment. Which Delta Lake feature enables this recovery?
options: OPTIMIZE — it compacts small files and rebuilds table statistics, selecting only data files that contain valid records.
VACUUM — run with a zero-hour retention period to immediately remove all files written after a target timestamp.

Time travel (RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF N or TIMESTAMP AS OF T) — Delta's transaction log records every write as a versioned snapshot, allowing the table to be restored to any prior version within the retention window.
Schema evolution — enable mergeSchema on a corrective write to roll back type changes introduced by the faulty deployment.
questions.explanation: Delta Lake maintains a transaction log in the _delta_log directory that records every write operation as a versioned JSON entry, creating an ordered history of all table changes. The RESTORE TABLE command uses this log to roll the table back to any prior state still within the retention window, making all subsequent queries see the pre-corruption data. OPTIMIZE is a compaction command that merges small Parquet files into larger ones for query performance — it selects files by size, not data validity, and cannot remove corrupted rows. VACUUM permanently deletes Parquet files older than the retention threshold; running it against a target timestamp removes historical data but cannot restore a table state. Schema evolution with mergeSchema adds new columns to an existing schema but does not revert data values or remove corrupted rows. Practical takeaway: after any production deployment that modifies a critical Delta table, record the last-known-good version number so that a RESTORE command can be executed immediately if validation fails.

questions.stem: True or False: The Delta Sharing open protocol allows a data provider to share live Delta tables with external consumers — who may be using Spark, pandas, or Power BI — without physically copying or moving the underlying Parquet data files; the consumer reads through a sharing server that issues pre-signed, time-limited URLs pointing to the relevant files in the provider's storage.
questions.explanation: TRUE. Delta Sharing, originally developed by Databricks and now governed by the Linux Foundation as an open standard, enables zero-copy cross-organisation data sharing. A sharing server sits in front of the provider's cloud storage. When a consumer requests data, the server validates credentials and returns pre-signed, time-limited URLs pointing directly to the relevant Parquet files in the provider's storage — no data is physically copied or moved to a consumer-owned location. Compatible clients exist for Apache Spark, Python pandas, Power BI, and other tools. The provider retains full control: data lives in their storage, access is token-scoped, and credentials can be revoked at any time. The consumer always reads the current version of the data, not a stale copy. This architecture eliminates the cost, latency, and staleness risk of maintaining ETL pipelines that export data to partner systems. Practical takeaway: evaluate Delta Sharing before building custom export pipelines to external partners — it is the standard for Lakehouse-native data sharing.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a daily MERGE to upsert 100,000 changed records into a 500-million-row Delta table keyed on customer_id. Despite only 100K rows changing, the job consistently takes 3 hours. Inspection of the Spark query plan shows the MERGE is doing a full scan of the 500M-row target table. What is the most effective fix?
options: Add a partition filter on a date column to restrict the MERGE target to recent partitions — limiting the scan to only the partitions that could realistically contain today's changed rows rather than the full 500M-row table.
Delta Lake does not natively support MERGE on high-cardinality keys like customer_id; replace the MERGE with a DELETE-then-INSERT pattern to avoid this known full-table-scan limitation.
Increase the executor count — the MERGE runs on the driver node and adding more workers will reduce execution time proportionally.
Replace customer_id with a hashed surrogate key; Delta's internal hash-join processes these in O(n) time, eliminating the full scan.
questions.explanation: When a MERGE statement targets a large table, the engine must find which target rows match each source row. Without any filter, it scans every partition in the target — all 500M rows — because it has no information to eliminate non-matching partitions. Adding a partition filter (e.g., WHERE target.updated_date >= current_date - 7) tells the engine to restrict the target scan to the recent partitions that could plausibly contain today's changed records, reducing the scan from 500M rows to perhaps 5M. This is the single highest-impact optimisation for large Delta MERGE operations. Delta Lake does natively support MERGE on any key including high-cardinality ones, so option B is incorrect. MERGE executes as a distributed Spark job across all workers — it is not driver-bound — so option C is wrong. Hashing the join key does not eliminate the need to scan the target table, making option D ineffective. Practical takeaway: always pair large MERGE operations with a date-bounded partition filter on the target; the performance difference can be an order of magnitude.

questions.stem: A team is migrating their Lakehouse to a multi-cloud environment using Trino on AWS, Snowflake on Azure, and Spark on GCP. They need a single open table format that all three engines can read natively without requiring vendor-specific connectors. Which table format best satisfies this multi-engine, multi-cloud requirement in 2026?
options: Delta Lake — it has the broadest enterprise community and Databricks' commercial backing ensures all major engines prioritise Delta compatibility.

Apache Iceberg — designed as an open, engine-agnostic standard governed by the Apache Software Foundation; Trino, Snowflake, Spark, AWS Athena, and Google BigQuery all provide native first-class Iceberg support without requiring vendor-specific connectors.
Both formats are now equivalent for this use case — Delta and Iceberg reached full multi-engine parity in 2026 and all major engines support both identically.
ORC — as the original open columnar format from the Hive ecosystem, it is the most universally compatible choice across all query engines.
questions.explanation: Apache Iceberg was designed from inception as an open, engine-agnostic table format with no single vendor controlling the specification. It is governed by the Apache Software Foundation and has native first-class integrations across every major query engine: Trino, Apache Spark, Snowflake, AWS Athena, Google BigQuery, Apache Flink, and Hive all support Iceberg without custom connectors. This makes Iceberg the natural choice for multi-cloud, multi-engine environments where avoiding lock-in is an explicit requirement. Delta Lake has excellent Databricks ecosystem support and is the right choice for organisations standardising on Databricks, but cross-engine support has historically required Databricks-specific connectors. By 2026 Unity Catalog improved Delta's multi-engine story, but Iceberg retains the stronger open-governance and cross-cloud portability narrative. ORC was designed for Hive and HDFS and lacks first-class cloud-native support. Practical takeaway: choose table format based on your engine ecosystem and vendor-lock-in requirements — Iceberg for multi-cloud portability, Delta for Databricks-centric platforms.

questions.stem: A Databricks Delta table is partitioned by transaction_date (730 distinct values over 2 years) and store_id (200 values). Analysts query exclusively by product_category (50 distinct values), which is not a partition key. Each such query opens all 146,000 partition directories before filtering. A teammate proposes replacing the partition scheme with Liquid Clustering on product_category. What is the primary advantage Liquid Clustering provides in this scenario?
options: Liquid Clustering enforces a unique constraint on product_category, preventing duplicate category assignments that cause the current partition fan-out.
Liquid Clustering is a write-time buffering optimisation that reduces file fragmentation — achieving the same outcome as repartitioning by product_category but without requiring table downtime.
Liquid Clustering achieves better compression ratios by interleaving high-cardinality and low-cardinality column values within the same Parquet file block.

Liquid Clustering physically co-locates rows with similar product_category values across Parquet files and records file-level statistics in the Delta log, enabling the query engine to skip files that don't contain the queried category — without imposing a rigid directory hierarchy, and it adapts incrementally as new data arrives without a full table rewrite.
questions.explanation: Traditional partitioning forces the engine to traverse every directory combination when the query predicate doesn't include all partition columns — a table with 730 dates × 200 stores has 146,000 partitions, all opened for a product_category-only query. Liquid Clustering (Databricks Runtime 13.3+) co-locates rows with similar clustering column values across a flat set of Parquet files and records min/max statistics for those columns in the Delta transaction log. At query time, the engine consults these file-level statistics to skip files that cannot contain the queried category, achieving effective data skipping without directory traversal overhead. Critically, Liquid Clustering is incremental: new data is clustered as it arrives without requiring a full table rewrite, unlike traditional repartitioning which rebuilds the entire table. Practical takeaway: Liquid Clustering is Databricks' recommended approach for most Delta tables; use explicit partitioning only when the partition column is always present in query predicates and has stable, bounded cardinality.

questions.stem: A logistic regression model predicts employee churn (Yes/No) and outputs a probability of 0.68 for a specific employee. The production system requires a binary decision. The team must select a classification threshold. Which statement best describes how the threshold should be chosen?
options: The threshold is a tunable parameter; setting it lower (e.g., 0.3) increases recall by flagging more employees as at-risk but also increases false positives; the optimal threshold depends on the relative business cost of missing a churner versus triggering unnecessary HR outreach.
The threshold is always fixed at 0.5 — this is mathematically implied by the sigmoid function's symmetric output range.
The threshold should be set to maximise accuracy, which statistical theory shows is optimal at 0.5 for logistic regression on balanced training data.
The threshold must equal the base rate of churn in the training population to ensure predictions are properly calibrated.
questions.explanation: The sigmoid function maps any real input to a 0–1 probability. Converting this probability to a binary decision requires a threshold — above which the model predicts Yes, below which No. The threshold is a design choice, not a fixed mathematical property. In churn prediction, missing a churner (false negative) typically costs far more than an unnecessary retention outreach (false positive): replacing a churned employee can cost 50–200% of annual salary, while an unnecessary HR conversation costs a manager's time. This asymmetry justifies a lower threshold to maximise recall of at-risk employees. Precision becomes the priority in scenarios where false positives carry high costs — for example, blocking a legitimate customer transaction. Neither 'maximise accuracy' nor 'use the class base rate' are principled threshold selection strategies. The ROC curve and Precision-Recall curve provide visual tools for selecting the threshold that best matches the problem's cost tradeoff. Practical takeaway: connect threshold selection to a business cost conversation, not to a statistical convention.

questions.stem: True or False: Logistic regression is correctly classified as a regression algorithm because it uses a linear equation as its core computation — the same mathematical operation used by linear regression.
questions.explanation: FALSE. Despite its name, logistic regression is a classification algorithm. The confusion is historical: logistic regression passes a linear combination of input features through the sigmoid function to produce a probability between 0 and 1, then thresholds that probability to produce a categorical class label. The output is a discrete class prediction, not a continuous numeric value — which is the defining characteristic of a classifier. Linear regression, by contrast, predicts a continuous numeric value directly as a weighted sum of inputs with no sigmoid step. The rule is simple: look at the target variable's type. If the target is continuous (house prices, revenue), the task is regression. If the target is categorical (churn/no-churn, fraud/not-fraud, customer tier A/B/C), the task is classification. Logistic regression uses a regression-like linear equation internally, but its purpose and output type are classification. Practical takeaway: classify algorithms by what they predict, not by the mathematical operations they perform internally.

questions.stem: A team trains a customer lifetime value (CLV) model using features including total_purchases_12m, days_since_signup, and avg_order_value_30d. The model scores R² = 0.96 on the held-out test set. After deployment, production predictions are far worse. A senior data scientist suspects a data integrity issue in the feature pipeline. Which scenario most likely explains the performance collapse?
options: R² of 0.96 signals overfitting — the model memorised training noise; adding stronger regularisation will close the train-to-production gap.

A feature derived from or temporally correlated with the future CLV target was included in training — for example, avg_order_value_30d may overlap the measurement window used to compute CLV, allowing the model to partially observe the answer during training and test but not in production.
The training dataset was too large — models trained on very large populations memorise noise rather than patterns and generalise poorly.
R² is not a valid metric for customer lifetime value; switching to RMSE as the primary metric would reveal the true performance gap.
questions.explanation: Data leakage occurs when a training feature contains information derived from or temporally correlated with the future target — information unavailable at actual prediction time. avg_order_value_30d is a classic leakage candidate: if CLV is measured over a 90-day window and this feature is computed over the last 30 days of that same period, the model can indirectly observe part of the value it is supposed to predict. Both training and test sets share this leakage, producing inflated scores across the board — but production data has no such overlap because predictions are made before the target window closes. A legitimate R² of 0.96 on noisy real-world CLV data should itself trigger suspicion; real CLV models rarely achieve such high scores. The fix is strict temporal splitting: compute every feature using data that predates the prediction cutoff by the full measurement horizon, and validate this with an explicit feature-window timeline. Practical takeaway: draw a timeline diagram of feature windows and target windows before building any feature pipeline — temporal overlap is the most common and most damaging modelling error.

questions.stem: A data scientist compares a single decision tree (96% train accuracy, 59% test accuracy) with a random forest (88% train accuracy, 86% test accuracy) on a churn classification task. Which statement best explains why the random forest generalises significantly better despite lower training accuracy?
options: The random forest underfits — lower training accuracy is always a signal of a weaker, more conservatively regularised model.
The random forest trains on a smaller total dataset because bootstrap sampling excludes some rows, so it encounters less noise and avoids the memorisation that the single tree exhibits.
Adding more trees to the random forest would bring its training accuracy up to 96% while simultaneously improving test accuracy beyond 86%.

The random forest uses bagging: each tree trains on a random bootstrap sample of rows with a random subset of features, creating diverse trees whose individual overfitting patterns cancel in the majority vote — producing an ensemble with far lower variance and better generalisation than any single overfit tree.
questions.explanation: A single decision tree can grow arbitrarily deep, memorising every training example including noise and idiosyncratic outliers — the 37-percentage-point train-test gap confirms this overfitting. Random forests counter this through two complementary mechanisms. First, each tree trains on a bootstrap sample (random subset of training rows, sampled with replacement), so no tree sees all the data and each learns slightly different patterns. Second, each split considers only a random subset of features, preventing all trees from converging on the same dominant predictors. When many diverse trees vote together, individual overfitting patterns cancel out and the ensemble's prediction is dominated by signal rather than noise — producing lower variance and better generalisation. The 88% training accuracy is not a weakness; it reflects that the ensemble resists fitting noise the single tree memorised. Lower training accuracy paired with higher test accuracy is exactly the expected signature of a properly regularised ensemble. Practical takeaway: a train-test accuracy gap exceeding 15 percentage points is a reliable overfit signal; random forests and gradient boosting are the standard remedies.

questions.stem: True or False: A model predicting house sale prices in dollars is a classification task, while a model predicting whether a mortgage applicant will default (Yes or No) is a regression task.
questions.explanation: FALSE. The task type is determined by the shape of the target variable — what the model predicts, not the techniques used internally. House sale prices in dollars produce continuous numeric outputs, which makes this a regression task. Predicting whether a mortgage applicant will default produces one of two discrete categorical labels (Yes or No), which makes this a classification task. Getting this distinction wrong has immediate practical consequences: applying a regression model to a binary target produces outputs that can fall outside 0–1 and cannot be directly interpreted as probabilities or class memberships. Applying a classification model to a continuous target loses the continuous ordering information the task requires. A reliable test: is the output a number on a continuous scale (regression) or a label drawn from a fixed set of categories (classification)? The choice of task type determines the model family, loss function, and evaluation metrics. Practical takeaway: define and document the target variable type at the start of every modelling project — every downstream decision follows from this single choice.

questions.stem: A procurement manager uses the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula to set replenishment sizes for a seasonal product. Her manager questions whether EOQ is appropriate for this use case. Which EOQ assumption, if violated, most directly invalidates the formula's result?
options: EOQ assumes demand arrives in discrete weekly batches; if demand is continuous, the formula systematically understates the optimal order size.

EOQ assumes demand rate, ordering cost, and holding cost are constant over time; if any of these vary significantly — such as seasonal demand spikes, variable freight rates, or supplier volume discounts — the formula produces an order quantity that no longer minimises total inventory cost.
EOQ assumes the organisation maintains 100% in-stock availability and can only be applied to products that have never experienced a stockout.
EOQ is only valid for single-warehouse operations; multi-location businesses must use distributed EOQ variants with warehouse-specific cost parameters.
questions.explanation: EOQ minimises total inventory cost by balancing ordering cost (the cost of placing and receiving each replenishment order) against holding cost (the cost of carrying one unit for one period). The formula is derived under the explicit assumption that demand rate, ordering cost, and holding cost are constant over time. When demand is seasonal (a retailer whose Q4 demand is 3× its Q1 demand), a single average demand rate produces an order quantity that is simultaneously too small in peak periods and too large in slow periods. Variable freight costs (spot market surcharges, fuel adjustments) make ordering cost dynamic. Supplier volume discounts make the effective cost-per-unit a function of order quantity, introducing nonlinearity EOQ cannot handle. In any of these scenarios, more appropriate models include dynamic lot-sizing (Wagner-Whitin) or simulation-based optimisation. EOQ remains a useful first-approximation benchmark but must be validated against actual cost data before operationalisation. Practical takeaway: always verify whether EOQ's constant-parameter assumptions hold for your specific product category before using the formula to drive operational replenishment decisions.

questions.stem: True or False: Setting a uniform 2-week safety-stock buffer for every SKU in a product catalogue is an efficient inventory policy because it applies the same proportional risk protection across all products.
questions.explanation: FALSE. Appropriate safety-stock levels depend on three SKU-specific inputs: demand variability (how unpredictably consumption fluctuates), supplier lead time and its variability (how reliably deliveries arrive), and the organisation's service-level target (how often it is willing to accept a stockout). A flat rule ignores all three. A high-volume, stable-demand product from a local supplier with a 2-day lead time needs minimal safety stock — 2 weeks of buffer ties up working capital without proportionate protection. A slow-moving, volatile component from an overseas supplier with 8-week lead times and high variability may require far more than 2 weeks to maintain even a 90% service level. The correct formula is: safety stock = Z × σ_demand × √(lead time), where Z reflects the target service level and σ_demand is per-SKU demand standard deviation. A flat rule simultaneously over-protects stable items (wasting capital) and under-protects volatile items (risking stockouts). Practical takeaway: segment SKUs by demand variability and lead time variability first — a flat safety-stock rule is almost certainly wrong for both extremes of the distribution.

questions.stem: An electronics manufacturer implements Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory management, reducing safety stock to near zero across its supply chain. Eight months later, an earthquake disrupts a key supplier for 3 weeks, triggering production halts that cost 10× the annual savings from the JIT programme. Which principle does this scenario most directly illustrate?
options: JIT optimises for cost efficiency under stable supply conditions by eliminating the inventory buffer that absorbs disruptions; this makes the system highly fragile to supply-side shocks — the resilience-vs-efficiency tradeoff intrinsic to the strategy, not a failure of its execution.
JIT requires highly accurate demand forecasting; the earthquake made consumer demand erratic and invalidated the forecast models that underpin JIT scheduling.
JIT is an automotive manufacturing practice that does not generalise to electronics supply chains, which have more volatile component availability.
The correct JIT implementation includes emergency procurement contracts with backup suppliers; the root cause was the operational failure to activate those contracts during the disruption.
questions.explanation: JIT eliminates the buffer stock that exists precisely to absorb supply disruptions. In stable operating conditions this reduces working capital, warehouse costs, and obsolescence — the efficiency case is real and validated by decades of automotive practice. The cost is resilience: when supply is disrupted for any reason — natural disaster, port closure, factory fire, geopolitical event — there is no buffer to absorb it and production halts immediately. This is not a failure of JIT execution; it is a direct consequence of the tradeoff the strategy explicitly makes. Toyota experienced exactly this dynamic during the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake. Modern supply chain design addresses it by applying JIT selectively — to stable, commodity components with multiple qualified sources — while maintaining safety stock for single-sourced, long-lead-time, or critical-path items. Practical takeaway: before implementing JIT across any supply chain, map every single-source dependency and stress-test the scenario where that supplier is unavailable for 4–6 weeks; the total expected cost must include disruption risk, not just holding-cost savings.

questions.stem: A data engineering team is asked to enable per-SKU safety-stock optimisation. After discovery, they identify that the team cannot calculate accurate safety-stock levels because the required inputs are missing or stale. Which pipeline deliverable most directly unlocks this capability?
options: A real-time dashboard showing current warehouse stock levels across all locations, giving operations teams live visibility to monitor inventory.
A machine learning model that predicts which SKUs will experience a stockout in the next 7 days, enabling proactive replenishment orders.
An event-driven alert pipeline that fires a Slack notification whenever any SKU drops below a configurable threshold, replacing the manual stock-monitoring process.

A reliable, freshly-refreshed pipeline that provides per-SKU demand variability (standard deviation of historical demand), average supplier lead time, and lead time variability — the three core inputs that safety-stock formulas require, at the product-level granularity needed for each category tier.
questions.explanation: Safety-stock formulas are mathematically straightforward — the standard formula is: safety stock = Z × σ_demand × √(lead time) — but they produce valid outputs only when the inputs are accurate and current. The three essential inputs are: per-SKU demand variability (standard deviation of historical demand), average supplier lead time, and lead time variability (standard deviation of delivery times). Without reliable data for these inputs, any safety-stock calculation is wrong regardless of formula correctness. A dashboard (option A) provides visibility into current stock levels but not the statistical inputs for calculation. A predictive ML model (option B) is a downstream application that depends on the same foundational data. An alert pipeline (option C) is reactive, not analytical. Data engineering's role here is foundational: build the pipelines that make the required inputs accurate, freshly refreshed, and accessible at the right granularity. Once the inputs exist, the formulas are straightforward to apply. Practical takeaway: enabling inventory optimisation almost always means building better data feeds, not building the optimisation model itself.

questions.stem: A supply planner calculates the reorder point for a product. Average weekly demand is 50 units, supplier lead time is 3 weeks, and the target safety stock is 1 week of average demand. What is the correct reorder point?
options: 50 units — the reorder point equals one week of demand, reflecting the safety stock buffer alone.
150 units — lead time demand only (3 weeks × 50 units = 150); safety stock is a separate reserve tracked independently and must not be folded into the reorder point calculation.
200 units — lead time demand (3 × 50 = 150) plus safety stock (1 × 50 = 50), triggering the replenishment order while enough buffer remains to absorb any lead time overrun.
250 units — double the lead time demand as a conservative buffer against simultaneous demand spikes and supplier delays.
questions.explanation: The reorder point (ROP) is the inventory level at which a replenishment order must be placed so that stock arrives before existing inventory is exhausted. The formula is: ROP = (Average demand per period × Lead time) + Safety stock. Substituting: ROP = (50 × 3) + (50 × 1) = 150 + 50 = 200 units. The logic: when inventory reaches 200 units, an order is placed and will arrive after 3 weeks. Expected demand during that lead time consumes 150 units. The remaining 50 units of safety stock absorb demand spikes or a short supplier delay without a stockout. Option A (50 units) omits both lead time demand and safety stock. Option B (150 units) correctly calculates lead time demand but excludes safety stock — placing the order at 150 means safety stock is already consumed when the new order arrives. Safety stock is explicitly part of the ROP formula because it must still be present when the replenishment arrives. Option D (250 units) has no standard formula basis. Practical takeaway: the reorder point and safety stock are inseparable — safety stock sets the floor you must still have on hand when your replenishment order arrives.

questions.stem: A data analyst writes the following SQL: `SELECT revenue * 0.9 AS discounted, region FROM sales WHERE discounted > 1000`. The query fails with 'column not found'. What is the most accurate explanation?
options: The column 'revenue' cannot be used inside a SELECT expression if it will also be aliased.
SQL evaluates WHERE before SELECT, so the alias 'discounted' does not yet exist when the filter is applied.
Arithmetic expressions inside SELECT are not permitted when a WHERE clause is present.
WHERE clauses cannot reference columns that appear in the same SELECT list.
questions.explanation: SQL has a logical processing order that differs from the written order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. Because WHERE is evaluated before SELECT, any column alias defined in the SELECT list does not exist at the time the WHERE filter runs. The alias 'discounted' is unknown to the database engine when it processes `WHERE discounted > 1000`. The fix is to repeat the expression (`WHERE revenue * 0.9 > 1000`) or use a subquery or CTE that wraps the SELECT and filters in an outer query. This is a frequent source of bugs for developers moving from application code — where variables are evaluated left-to-right — to SQL. Options A and C misstate how SELECT parsing works; option D is too broad and incorrect.

questions.stem: True or False: When two rows share the same ORDER BY value in a RANK() window function, both receive the same rank number and the next assigned rank skips accordingly — for example, two rows tied for rank 2 both show 2, and the next row shows 4.
questions.explanation: This is the defining behavior of RANK(): tied rows get the same rank, and ranks are not consecutive after a tie — the gap reflects how many rows shared that rank. This distinguishes RANK() from DENSE_RANK(), which also assigns the same rank to ties but does not skip — the next rank after two rows tied at 2 would be 3, not 4. ROW_NUMBER(), by contrast, assigns a unique sequential integer to every row regardless of ties, producing non-deterministic ordering when values are equal. Choosing between these three functions is a common interview topic because the behavior differs in subtle but business-important ways, particularly in 'top N per group' queries.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs: `SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM refunds)`. The refunds table contains exactly one row where customer_id IS NULL. How many rows does this query return?
options: All orders except those belonging to customers who have a refund record.
Zero rows — NOT IN with a NULL-containing subquery evaluates to UNKNOWN for every comparison, so no row passes the filter.
An error — NULL values in a subquery used with NOT IN are rejected by the query planner.
The same result as NOT EXISTS, because NULL handling is identical between the two constructs.
questions.explanation: This is one of the most dangerous NULL traps in SQL. The predicate `x NOT IN (a, b, NULL)` expands to `x <> a AND x <> b AND x <> NULL`. Because any comparison with NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN (not TRUE), the entire predicate evaluates to UNKNOWN for every row — meaning no rows pass the filter. The result is zero rows returned, even though most of the refunds table has nothing to do with the NULL. The safe alternative is NOT EXISTS with a correlated subquery, which correctly handles NULLs by testing row existence rather than value equality. In production data this bug is particularly insidious because the NULL may be rare, causing queries to pass all tests yet silently return no data once a single NULL enters the table.

questions.stem: A team is migrating a T-SQL query `SELECT TOP 100 * FROM events ORDER BY event_time DESC` to Spark SQL. Which rewrite is correct?
options: SELECT FIRST 100 * FROM events ORDER BY event_time DESC
SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY event_time DESC LIMIT 100
SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY event_time DESC FETCH FIRST 100 ROWS ONLY
SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY event_time DESC TOP 100
questions.explanation: Spark SQL follows ANSI SQL syntax more closely than T-SQL. The equivalent of T-SQL's `TOP N` is the `LIMIT N` clause, placed at the end of the query after ORDER BY. Option A (`FIRST 100`) is not valid syntax in either Spark SQL or T-SQL. Option C (`FETCH FIRST 100 ROWS ONLY`) is valid ANSI SQL and works in databases like PostgreSQL and DB2 but is not supported in Spark SQL. Option D is not valid SQL anywhere — TOP is a T-SQL keyword that always appears immediately after SELECT, never at the end. Cross-platform SQL migration is a common task when moving workloads between SQL Server and Azure Databricks or BigQuery, and syntax differences like TOP vs LIMIT are among the first things to address in a migration script.

questions.stem: True or False: Adding `SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region)` to a SELECT statement collapses the result set to one row per region, the same way `GROUP BY region` does.
questions.explanation: Window functions do not reduce the number of rows — that is the fundamental distinction between window functions and aggregate functions with GROUP BY. `SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region)` computes the regional total but returns that value on every individual row within the partition. The original row count is preserved: if a region has 500 transactions, all 500 rows are returned, each showing the same regional total alongside its own transaction details. This makes window functions ideal for enriching row-level data with aggregate context — for example, computing each sale's share of its regional total — without losing the granularity that GROUP BY would eliminate. Confusion between the two constructs is a common source of incorrect reports.

questions.stem: A team builds a sales fact table with grain 'one row per invoice'. A developer later joins it directly to a daily-revenue summary table (grain: one row per day per region) without creating any bridging logic. What problem does this introduce?
options: The query will raise a schema error because the two tables have incompatible grain.
Revenue will be double-counted because each invoice row joins to a daily aggregate that already includes all invoices in that day.
Dimension keys will become NULL because surrogate key lookups do not cross grain boundaries.
Star schema queries with mixed-grain tables always return NULL for measure columns.
questions.explanation: Grain is the most important design decision in fact table design — it defines exactly what one row represents. When two tables with different grains are joined, the database cannot know you intend only partial matching. Each invoice row matches one daily-summary row, and that summary row already contains the total of all invoices for that day. The join causes every invoice to 'see' the full day's revenue, so SUM over the joined dataset multiplies each invoice's revenue by the number of invoices in that day — classic double-counting. The correct approach is either to design both fact tables at the same grain or to aggregate the invoice-level table before joining. Options C and D describe behaviors that do not occur in SQL databases.

questions.stem: True or False: A snowflake schema, where dimension tables are further normalized into sub-dimension tables, generally delivers better analytical query performance than a star schema on modern columnar data warehouses.
questions.explanation: The snowflake schema was designed to reduce data redundancy and storage by normalizing dimension tables, but this comes at a query-performance cost. Analytical queries against a snowflake schema require more joins to traverse the hierarchy of sub-dimension tables, which increases query complexity and latency. Modern columnar data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks) are optimized for wide, denormalized tables and large sequential scans — storage is cheap and joins across many tables are relatively expensive. The star schema's deliberately denormalized, wide dimensions reduce join depth and simplify query plans. Snowflake schemas may still be chosen for writability or storage constraints, but raw analytical performance favors the star schema in virtually all modern warehouse environments.

questions.stem: A data engineer proposes using the source CRM's customer_id as the primary key in the customer dimension table. A senior engineer objects. Which concern best justifies using a surrogate key instead?
options: Natural keys are always too long to index efficiently in columnar databases.
Source system keys can change, be reused after deletion, or be merged when systems consolidate — surrogate keys remain stable across these events.
Surrogate keys compress more efficiently than natural keys in Parquet and ORC file formats.
Data warehouse regulations require integer primary keys for compliance auditing.
questions.explanation: The core justification for surrogate keys is insulation from source system volatility. Natural keys from operational systems are designed for transactional integrity, not for long-term warehouse stability. Common problems include: customer IDs being recycled after account deletion, ID sequences resetting when a CRM is migrated, and two customer records merging into one after a company acquisition. A surrogate key — typically a system-generated integer or UUID assigned at load time — never changes regardless of what happens in the source system. This enables accurate history tracking in slowly changing dimensions and prevents broken foreign key relationships when source IDs shift. Options A and C are untrue technical claims; option D misrepresents the nature of compliance requirements.

questions.stem: An order fact table must capture three different dates — order date, ship date, and delivery date — and each date must support filtering and grouping by year, quarter, month, and day-of-week. What is the recommended dimensional modeling approach?
options: Add three VARCHAR columns (order_date, ship_date, delivery_date) directly to the fact table and handle date parsing in reports.
Create three separate physical date dimension tables (dim_order_date, dim_ship_date, dim_delivery_date), each with a full calendar hierarchy.
Create one physical date dimension table and reference it three times in the fact table under three different foreign key aliases — a role-playing dimension.
Store all three dates in a single junk dimension alongside low-cardinality order flags.
questions.explanation: A role-playing dimension is the correct pattern here. A single physical date dimension table (dim_date) contains the full calendar hierarchy and is referenced by the fact table through three separate foreign keys — for example, order_date_key, ship_date_key, and delivery_date_key — each aliased as a different 'role' in queries (e.g., `JOIN dim_date AS order_cal ON f.order_date_key = order_cal.date_key`). This avoids maintaining three identical tables that would need to be updated in sync whenever the calendar table is modified. Option A loses the calendar hierarchy entirely. Option B duplicates the full calendar table three times, creating a maintenance burden without benefit. Option D conflates the junk dimension pattern (for low-cardinality flags) with date handling.

questions.stem: True or False: In an accumulating snapshot fact table, each business process instance (e.g., an order lifecycle) is represented by exactly one row, and that row is updated in place as the process moves through each milestone.
questions.explanation: The accumulating snapshot fact table is one of Kimball's four fact table types and is purpose-built for processes with a defined beginning, end, and a series of milestones in between (order entry → picking → shipping → delivery). Each process instance gets one row, which is revisited and updated each time a new milestone is reached — replacing NULL date keys with actual date keys and incrementing lag measures (days between stages). This is different from a transaction fact table, where each business event appends a new row, and from a periodic snapshot, which captures point-in-time state at regular intervals. The update-in-place pattern can complicate CDC (change data capture) and warehouse loading logic but provides a compact, query-friendly view of process lifecycles.

questions.stem: A data platform monitors schema changes, pipeline freshness, and row count across all tables. All three indicators remain green, but an analytics team reports the revenue dashboard is down 30% with no corresponding business event. Which monitoring category would most likely have caught this first?
options: Schema drift monitoring — a column type change altered how revenue is aggregated.
Lineage monitoring — tracing the data's path would surface the upstream issue.
Distribution monitoring — tracking statistical properties such as mean and percentile ranges of the revenue column would flag the unexpected shift.
Freshness monitoring — the pipeline serving the revenue table may have stopped refreshing silently.
questions.explanation: Data observability covers four pillars: volume (row count), freshness (last updated), schema (structure), and distribution (statistical properties of values). In this scenario, volume, freshness, and schema are all green — meaning the pipeline ran, the row count is normal, and no columns changed. What changed is the distribution of values: revenue amounts shifted significantly. Distribution monitoring — which tracks metrics such as mean, median, percentile ranges, and null rates for key numeric columns — would have alerted the team when the rolling average of daily revenue fell outside historical bounds. Lineage monitoring answers 'where does this data come from?' but does not independently detect value-level anomalies. The scenario rules out schema and freshness as root causes, and volume alone would not capture a shift in value magnitudes.

questions.stem: True or False: Best practice for dbt CI pipelines is to run all models against the full production dataset on every pull request to ensure tests reflect realistic data volumes before code is merged.
questions.explanation: Running a full production build on every PR is expensive, slow, and potentially risky. The recommended pattern is 'slim CI': use dbt's state-based comparison (`--select state:modified+`) to build only the models that changed and their downstream dependents, running against a smaller, representative dataset (typically a dev schema or a sampled clone). This dramatically reduces CI run time from potentially hours to minutes, cuts compute costs, and avoids touching production data mid-PR. The trade-off is that performance at full scale is not validated in CI — that validation belongs in a staging environment before production deployment, not in per-PR checks. Running full production builds in CI also creates a blast radius where a bad PR could lock or degrade a shared environment during the CI run.

questions.stem: A data engineering team must validate quality on raw files ingested from an external partner. The schema changes frequently, and the team wants to automatically generate baseline quality expectations from existing data rather than writing assertions manually. Which tool best fits this requirement?
options: dbt tests — using a generic test suite covering not-null, unique, and accepted-values for every column.
Great Expectations — its data profiler generates expectation suites automatically from a sample of existing data, which can then be refined and enforced.
Soda — because it is the only tool that integrates directly with file-based data sources.
Monte Carlo — because its ML-based anomaly detection discovers all quality issues without any configuration.
questions.explanation: Great Expectations is uniquely suited to this use case because of its data profiler: it inspects a sample dataset and automatically generates a suite of expectations (column types, value ranges, null rates, unique value sets) that reflect what 'normal' looks like. When the schema changes, the profiler can be re-run to regenerate or update expectations. dbt tests require the schema to be known in advance — every test is written explicitly in YAML or SQL — making frequent schema changes a maintenance burden. Soda does integrate with file sources, but the claim that it is the only tool to do so is false. Monte Carlo focuses on continuous observability and anomaly detection in warehouse tables, not on profiling and validating raw file ingests. Great Expectations is the standard tool for expectation-driven, profiling-first quality validation.

questions.stem: A data platform team configures 200 data quality alerts across all pipelines. Within two weeks the on-call engineer begins dismissing most alerts without investigating. What is the root cause, and what is the recommended fix?
options: The alerting system is misconfigured — the fix is to raise alert thresholds so fewer alerts fire.
The team lacks a formal SLA — the fix is to mandate acknowledgment within one hour of every alert.
Alert fatigue caused by too many low-signal alerts — the fix is to ensure every alert is actionable, has a clear owner, and corresponds to a real business impact.
The monitoring pipeline itself is unreliable — the fix is to add redundancy to the alert delivery system.
questions.explanation: Alert fatigue occurs when the volume of alerts overwhelms the team's capacity to respond meaningfully, leading them to habituate to the noise and dismiss alerts by default. The root cause is not threshold misconfiguration or missing SLAs — it is that most alerts do not represent actionable problems with clear owners and business consequences. The recommended fix is to audit every alert against three questions: Is this alert actionable right now? Who owns the response? Does this represent a real business impact? Alerts that fail any test should be downgraded, disabled, or converted to dashboard signals rather than pages. Raising thresholds (option A) may reduce volume but does not address whether remaining alerts are meaningful. Option B adds process overhead without improving signal quality. Option D addresses a different problem.

questions.stem: True or False: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) allow data engineering teams to define Databricks workflows, job clusters, and permissions as YAML configuration files that can be version-controlled and deployed consistently across dev, staging, and production environments.
questions.explanation: Databricks Asset Bundles are the Databricks-native approach to infrastructure-as-code for data pipelines. A bundle defines jobs, clusters, notebooks, and Delta Live Tables pipelines in YAML, which can be stored in Git alongside the pipeline code. The Databricks CLI then deploys the bundle to a target environment by substituting environment-specific variables (such as cluster sizes or catalog names). This brings standard software engineering practices — version control, code review, automated deployment — to Databricks workloads. Before DABs, teams often relied on the Databricks REST API or third-party tools (Terraform providers, dbx) to achieve similar results. DABs represent Databricks' first-party answer to the question of how to manage workspace resources as code, analogous to how dbt manages transformation logic as code.

questions.stem: A data scientist clusters customers using two features: annual revenue (range $10,000–$5,000,000) and number of support tickets filed (range 0–50). Without any preprocessing, what problem will K-means clustering encounter?
options: K-means cannot process numeric features measured in different units within the same model.
The large revenue values will dominate the Euclidean distance calculation, making the support-ticket count nearly irrelevant to cluster assignments.
K-means requires all features to follow a normal distribution, which revenue data rarely satisfies.
The algorithm will assign all customers to a single cluster when feature ranges differ significantly.
questions.explanation: K-means uses Euclidean distance to measure similarity between points. When features have very different scales, distance is dominated by the feature with the largest values. In this example, a difference of $100,000 in revenue contributes enormously to the distance calculation, while a difference of 5 support tickets contributes almost nothing — even though both differences might be equally meaningful from a business perspective. The result is that cluster boundaries are effectively drawn only on the revenue axis, and the support-ticket feature is ignored in practice. The standard fix is to standardize features (e.g., z-score normalization or min-max scaling) before clustering so each feature contributes proportionally. Option A is false — units don't matter, scale does. Option C is a constraint for linear models, not K-means. Option D overstates the effect.

questions.stem: True or False: K-means clustering will always return exactly K non-empty cluster assignments, even when run on data that contains no meaningful groupings.
