questions.stem: A data engineer is asked to implement a Figma wireframe for a new executive dashboard. What is the most important step to take before writing any code?
options: Export all assets from Figma and set up the front-end project structure.
Validate that the required data sources exist, are reliable, and can support the metrics shown in the wireframe.
Schedule a demo with stakeholders to gather aesthetic feedback on the wireframe.
Select the charting library that best matches the visual style in the wireframe.
questions.explanation: Wireframes are aspirational and designed without knowledge of data constraints. The highest risk in dashboard implementation is discovering mid-build that a required metric cannot be computed from available data, requires a costly new pipeline, or has data quality issues that would make the metric meaningless. Validating data availability and reliability before writing any front-end code prevents expensive rework. Asset export and library selection (options A and D) are tactical steps that follow data validation. Stakeholder aesthetic feedback (option C) is useful but secondary to confirming feasibility.

questions.stem: Which UX principle most directly reduces cognitive overload on a complex data dashboard, improving the chance users engage with the content rather than leaving?
options: Progressive disclosure — surface high-level summaries first, with detail available on demand.
Colour coding — assign a distinct colour to each KPI to help users scan faster.
Data density — show all available metrics on a single screen to minimise navigation.
Animation — use live-updating charts to signal that data is fresh.
questions.explanation: Progressive disclosure is the principle of initially presenting only the most important information and hiding detail behind interactions (drill-downs, tooltips, expandable sections). This reduces cognitive overload by not overwhelming users with complexity upfront, while still keeping detail accessible. Data density (option C) is the opposite — cramming all metrics onto one screen is a common cause of dashboard abandonment. Colour coding helps scanning but does not reduce overload when the content volume itself is high. Animation (option D) can help signal freshness but adds distraction if overused and does not address information volume.

questions.stem: True or False: Using distinct colours alone (without shape, pattern, or label differences) is sufficient to make a data visualisation accessible to all users.
questions.explanation: Approximately 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of colour vision deficiency, with red-green colour blindness being the most common. Relying on colour alone means these users cannot distinguish encoded data categories. WCAG accessibility guidelines require that colour is not the sole means of conveying information. Accessible data visualisations combine colour with secondary cues — distinct shapes, patterns, textures, or direct labels — so that the same information is perceivable regardless of the viewer's colour vision.

questions.stem: A data engineering team is establishing a Definition of Done (DoD) for their sprint tickets. Which of the following acceptance criteria is most commonly omitted in early-stage teams, leading to production incidents?
options: Unit tests written and passing.
Code reviewed by at least one peer.
Integration or end-to-end tests covering the new data flow.
Feature branch merged to the main development branch.
questions.explanation: Early-stage data engineering teams typically adopt unit tests and code review as DoD criteria quickly because they are well-understood software engineering practices. Integration and end-to-end tests (option C) are often skipped because they are harder to write, require test infrastructure (realistic environments, seed data), and take longer to run. This gap means a piece of code can pass all unit tests and code review but still fail in production when it interacts with real external systems, upstream schema changes, or downstream consumers. The omission of integration tests is one of the most frequent root causes of data pipeline incidents.

questions.stem: A data scientist is using a gradient boosted tree (XGBoost) to predict customer churn. The `days_since_last_purchase` feature has a right-skewed distribution. What transformation should they apply?
options: Apply log(1 + x) to compress the skew before training.
Apply min-max normalisation to scale values between 0 and 1.
No transformation is needed — tree-based models are invariant to monotonic feature transformations.
Apply z-score standardisation to centre the distribution around zero.
questions.explanation: Tree-based models (decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting) split features at threshold values and are therefore invariant to any monotonic transformation of the input — log, square root, normalisation, and standardisation all leave the tree's optimal splits unchanged because rank order is preserved. These transformations are important for linear models, SVMs, and neural networks that assume or are sensitive to scale or distribution. Applying them to tree inputs adds complexity without benefit. Options A, B, and D are all valid preprocessing steps for models that are scale-sensitive, but are unnecessary here.

questions.stem: A feature for day-of-week is encoded as integers 0 (Monday) through 6 (Sunday). Why might this encoding be problematic for a linear model, and what is the preferred alternative?
options: Integers violate the independence assumption of linear models; use dummy variables instead.

The integer encoding implies a false linear ordering (Sunday=6 is not 'more day' than Monday=0) and also breaks the cyclical wrap-around (Sunday and Monday are adjacent). Use sine and cosine encoding of the angle instead.
Integers are not supported by most ML frameworks; use label encoding followed by PCA.
The encoding is fine for linear models; the issue only arises with neural networks.
questions.explanation: Encoding day-of-week as integers 0-6 implies a linear relationship where Sunday (6) is numerically far from Monday (0), but in reality they are adjacent days. Linear models will treat the numeric distance as meaningful. The standard solution is cyclical encoding: convert the integer to an angle (2*pi * day / 7) and then encode as both sin(angle) and cos(angle). This gives the model two continuous features that correctly represent the cyclic proximity — Sunday and Monday are close together in (sin, cos) space. One-hot / dummy encoding (option A) also works but produces 7 features. PCA on label encoding (option C) is not a standard or recommended pattern.

questions.stem: True or False: It is safe to compute target encoding (replacing a categorical feature with the mean target value per category) on the full training dataset before performing a train/validation split.
questions.explanation: Computing target encoding on the full training set before splitting leaks target information into the validation set. The validation rows' categories will have their own target values incorporated into the encoding mean, making the encoded feature artificially informative for those rows. The correct approach is to compute target encoding only on the training fold (using cross-fold target encoding or leave-one-out encoding within the training split), and then apply the resulting mapping to the validation/test set using only training-fold statistics. Leaky target encoding is a common source of overly optimistic validation metrics that fail to generalise to production.

questions.stem: Given a raw event timestamp, which set of derived features typically provides the most predictive signal for a fraud detection model?
options: epoch_seconds, epoch_milliseconds, ISO8601_string
year, month, day_of_month (as integers)
days_since_last_event (recency), hour_of_day (cyclical), is_weekend (binary)
unix_timestamp_log, sqrt_unix_timestamp, timestamp_z_score
questions.explanation: For fraud detection, the behavioural and temporal context encoded in derived features is far more predictive than the raw timestamp value. Recency (days_since_last_event) captures unusual gaps or bursts in activity. Hour_of_day (encoded cyclically) captures time-of-day behaviour patterns — fraudulent transactions often cluster at unusual hours. is_weekend captures a behavioural dimension correlated with reduced oversight. Options A and D are monotonic transformations of the raw timestamp that encode essentially the same information. Option B (year/month/day) captures calendar time but misses within-day and recency signals critical to fraud.

questions.stem: A Pearson correlation analysis between a candidate feature and the target variable returns r of approximately 0. What is the correct conclusion?
options: The feature has no relationship with the target and should be dropped.
The feature may still have a strong non-linear relationship with the target; use mutual information or a model-based importance score before discarding.
The feature has a perfect non-linear relationship with the target by definition.
Pearson r of 0 indicates the feature is collinear with another feature in the dataset.
questions.explanation: Pearson correlation measures only linear association. A feature with r near 0 can still have strong non-linear predictive power — for example, a quadratic relationship (y = x squared) produces r = 0 between x and y even though x perfectly determines y. Before discarding a feature based on Pearson r alone, assess it with non-linear measures such as Spearman rank correlation, mutual information (which captures any statistical dependency), or by checking feature importance in a tree-based model. Option C is incorrect — r = 0 does not imply non-linear perfection. Option D confuses correlation with collinearity.

questions.stem: A manufacturer relies on a single overseas supplier for a critical component. Which risk mitigation strategy most directly addresses the root cause of this vulnerability?
options: Increase safety stock to buffer against supply disruptions.
Implement real-time shipment tracking to detect disruptions earlier.
Negotiate longer lead-time commitments with the current supplier.
Qualify and onboard at least one alternative supplier for the component.
questions.explanation: The root cause of the vulnerability is single-source dependency — if the sole supplier is disrupted (geopolitical event, natural disaster, financial failure, quality issue), no buffer stock, better tracking, or contractual commitment changes that fundamental exposure. Supplier diversification (qualifying and onboarding an alternative source) directly eliminates single-source dependency, allowing production to switch suppliers during disruption. Increased safety stock (option A) delays the impact but does not prevent it. Real-time tracking (option B) improves response time but does not provide an alternative supply path. Longer lead-time commitments (option C) reduce scheduling uncertainty but do not add redundancy.

questions.stem: True or False: A risk heat map that ranks a risk as high-probability and high-impact always means that risk must be mitigated, regardless of the cost of mitigation.
questions.explanation: Risk heat maps prioritise risks by inherent severity (likelihood x impact) but do not account for the cost or feasibility of mitigation. Risk management decisions require a cost-benefit analysis: if the cost of mitigation exceeds the expected loss (probability x impact in monetary terms), the economically rational response may be to accept or transfer the risk (e.g., through insurance) rather than mitigate it. Heat maps are a triage tool that guides where to focus analysis, not a mandate to spend on mitigation. A high heat map position should trigger a cost-benefit review, not automatic mitigation spend.

questions.stem: A retailer notices that small fluctuations in consumer demand lead to increasingly large order swings at each upstream tier of their supply chain. What phenomenon does this describe?
options: Safety stock oscillation
The bullwhip effect
Demand smoothing
Lead-time compression
questions.explanation: The bullwhip effect (also called the Forrester effect) describes how small variations in end-consumer demand become progressively amplified as orders are passed upstream through the supply chain. Each tier (retailer to distributor to manufacturer to supplier) adds a buffer based on its own demand uncertainty, causing upstream order variability to far exceed actual consumer demand variability. Common causes include demand forecasting errors, order batching, price fluctuations, and shortage gaming. Mitigation strategies include sharing point-of-sale data upstream, reducing order batch sizes, stabilising pricing, and improving cross-tier demand visibility.

questions.stem: A demand forecasting team uses Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) to evaluate forecasts across thousands of SKUs. Which scenario will cause MAPE to produce misleading or undefined results?
options: SKUs with high seasonal variance in demand.
SKUs with negative demand values due to returns.
SKUs with zero or near-zero actual demand values.
SKUs with demand values greater than 10,000 units per period.
questions.explanation: MAPE divides the absolute forecast error by the actual value: |actual - forecast| / |actual|. When the actual demand is zero (or near-zero), this produces division by zero (undefined) or astronomically large percentage errors that dominate the average and make MAPE meaningless as a summary metric. This is a well-known limitation of MAPE for intermittent-demand or slow-moving SKUs. Alternatives include sMAPE (symmetric MAPE), MASE (Mean Absolute Scaled Error), or tracking metrics like fill rate and bias. Negative demand values (option B) also cause issues with percentage-based metrics but are less common. High-volume SKUs (option D) do not cause MAPE to fail.

questions.stem: True or False: Vertical integration — owning multiple tiers of the supply chain — eliminates supply chain risk for a company.
questions.explanation: Vertical integration reduces certain risks (external supplier dependency, information asymmetry between tiers) but introduces new ones and does not eliminate supply chain risk overall. Integrated operations still face demand uncertainty, natural disasters, regulatory changes, labour disruptions, and commodity price volatility. Additionally, vertical integration increases capital requirements, reduces flexibility to switch strategies, and concentrates operational risk within the company rather than distributing it across suppliers. It is a trade-off that may reduce specific external risks at the cost of increased internal complexity and new risk exposures.

questions.stem: During a system design interview for a large-scale data pipeline, what should you do before drawing any architecture diagram?
options: Start with the storage layer, as all data pipelines are storage-bound.
Clarify requirements: data volume, velocity, latency SLA, consistency requirements, and who the consumers are.
Propose a Lambda architecture, as it handles both batch and streaming use cases.
Ask the interviewer which cloud provider the company uses.
questions.explanation: System design interview performance is heavily weighted on structured thinking, and the first demonstration of that is clarifying ambiguous requirements before designing. Interviewers want to see that you do not jump to solutions without understanding constraints. Clarifying data volume, velocity, latency SLAs, and consumer requirements takes two minutes and prevents designing the wrong system. Proposing a specific architecture (option C) before understanding requirements is a common mistake — it signals pattern-matching rather than analytical thinking. Asking about cloud provider (option D) is relevant but secondary to functional requirements.

questions.stem: When answering a behavioural interview question using the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), where do most candidates spend too much time?
options: Action — they over-explain the technical steps they took.
Result — they spend too long quantifying the business impact.
Situation — they spend too long setting up the context before getting to what they did.
Task — they repeat the job description rather than the specific challenge.
questions.explanation: The most common STAR mistake is spending the majority of the answer on Situation — setting up the background story, the team context, the organisation structure — leaving too little time for Action and Result, which are the parts the interviewer actually cares about. The Situation and Task sections should together take no more than 20-30% of the response. Action (what you specifically did) and Result (the measurable outcome) should dominate. A useful rule: if your Situation section is longer than your Action section, you have the balance wrong.

questions.stem: A common SQL interview question asks for the second-highest salary from an employees table. Which query correctly handles duplicate salary values (returning NULL if fewer than two distinct salaries exist)?
options: SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1
SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)
SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1
SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 2
questions.explanation: Option B uses a subquery to find the highest salary, then takes the MAX of all salaries below that threshold — correctly returning the second-distinct salary value and returning NULL automatically if no such value exists (MAX of an empty set is NULL). Option A (OFFSET 1 without DISTINCT) fails when multiple employees share the top salary — it returns the same salary again, not the second-distinct value. Option C (DISTINCT + OFFSET) works for finding the second distinct value but does not return NULL when there is only one distinct salary — it returns no rows instead. Option D returns the top two rows, not just the second highest salary.

questions.stem: An interviewer asks 'Why do you want to be a data engineer rather than a data scientist or software engineer?' Which answer most effectively differentiates a strong candidate?
options: 'I enjoy working with large datasets and learning new tools like Spark and dbt.'
'I prefer backend systems over frontend development and data is interesting to me.'
'I am drawn to the challenge of making data reliably available at scale — the measurable correctness of pipelines and the direct business impact when analytics teams can finally trust their data.'
'Data engineering has strong job growth and good compensation compared to other engineering roles.'
questions.explanation: Strong interview answers for motivation questions demonstrate intrinsic alignment between personal values and the role's core function. Option C identifies the specific things that make data engineering distinct — reliability, measurable correctness, and the business impact of trusted data — and links them to personal motivation. This shows the candidate understands what data engineers actually do beyond the tools. Option A mentions tools, which are means not ends, and is too generic. Option B frames DE negatively relative to other roles. Option D cites extrinsic motivations (growth, pay) which raise concerns about long-term commitment and passion for the work.

questions.stem: A data team stores clickstream events in ADLS partitioned by `event_date` and `event_type`. After six months they have 7,200 folders (180 dates × 40 event types), each containing files averaging 80 KB. Delta Lake queries are noticeably slower than expected. What is the ROOT CAUSE?
options: Delta Lake's transaction log cannot handle more than 5,000 partitions and degrades past that threshold
The small files problem: 7,200 directories with 80 KB files causes Spark to spend more time on metadata enumeration and task scheduling than on actual data reads
ADLS Gen2 throttles hierarchical namespace write operations above 1,000 folders per hour
Delta Lake automatically disables Z-ordering when a table has more than 1,000 partitions
questions.explanation: The small files problem occurs when high-cardinality partition keys create thousands of tiny files. With 7,200 directories at 80 KB average, each file is far below the target 128–256 MB for Parquet/Delta. Spark must contact the ADLS listing API for every folder to discover files, then schedule a separate task per file — the overhead of metadata enumeration and task scheduling vastly exceeds what partition pruning saves. Option A is false: Delta Lake's transaction log handles arbitrarily many partitions without a hard limit. Option C is fabricated: ADLS Gen2 does not impose a folder-write throttle of that kind. Option D is also false: Delta Lake ACID guarantees and Z-ordering are unrelated to partition count. The fix is to reduce partition cardinality (e.g., remove `event_type` from the key) and run periodic OPTIMIZE to compact files into larger chunks.

questions.stem: True or False: In Databricks, Liquid Clustering allows you to change the clustering columns at any time using ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY without rewriting all existing data immediately.
questions.explanation: The statement is TRUE. Databricks Liquid Clustering, available in Delta Lake 3.x, decouples the clustering definition from a mandatory full rewrite. When you run ALTER TABLE t CLUSTER BY (new_col), Databricks updates the table metadata to record the new clustering columns but does not immediately rewrite existing data. Files are progressively reclustered during future OPTIMIZE runs, which can be scheduled as background maintenance. This contrasts sharply with traditional Hive-style storage partitioning, where changing the partition key requires dropping and recreating the entire table and rewriting all data. The operational flexibility of Liquid Clustering is its primary advantage: evolving query patterns no longer require expensive, disruptive migrations. The trade-off is that pruning effectiveness builds up gradually as OPTIMIZE passes recluster more files over time.

questions.stem: Which statement about Spark's `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` configuration is TRUE?
options: It sets the number of files Spark reads per task when loading a partitioned Parquet table from ADLS
It controls the number of output partitions produced after wide transformations such as groupBy, join, and distinct
Calling `repartition(n)` on a DataFrame overrides this setting and guarantees that all n output partitions contain exactly equal amounts of data
Spark shuffle partitions persist across SparkSession instances so that data does not need to be re-read between jobs
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` determines how many partitions Spark creates after a wide transformation — operations that require shuffling data across executors, such as groupBy, join, distinct, and window aggregations. The default is 200, which is often too large for small datasets (creating many tiny tasks) and too small for very large ones (causing data skew per partition). Option A is wrong: input partitioning when reading files is controlled differently, primarily by the number of files and Spark's parallelism settings, not this config. Option C is false: repartition(n) creates exactly n partitions by count, but it uses round-robin or hash distribution, which does not guarantee equal data volume per partition — skewed keys produce skewed partitions. Option D is false: Spark partitions are in-memory constructs scoped to a single job; they do not persist between SparkSession instances.

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a Delta Lake table for 2 years of retail transactions (150 store IDs). Queries almost always filter by date range. Which partitioning strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Partition by store_id only — 150 values gives clean, manageable separation per store
Partition by transaction_date (daily) — date-range queries dominate, so pruning by date eliminates the vast majority of files at the folder-listing stage
Partition by both transaction_date and store_id — double partitioning maximises pruning for all query patterns simultaneously
Apply no partitioning and rely solely on Z-ordering by store_id and transaction_date to optimise reads
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. When date-range queries dominate, partitioning by transaction_date (daily) is the standard choice: a query for 'last 7 days' scans at most 7 of 730 date folders. This is strong partition pruning at the file-listing stage before any data is read. Option A (store_id only) fails for date-range queries — every query must scan all 150 store directories regardless of the date filter. Option C (double partitioning) creates 150 × 730 = 109,500 directories averaging very few rows each — a severe small files problem that costs more in metadata overhead than it saves in pruning. Option D (Z-ordering only, no partitioning) is a valid modern Databricks approach for some use cases, but Z-ordering works within files, not across directory listings; it cannot replace the folder-level skip that date partitioning provides when 95%+ of files can be eliminated before reading.

questions.stem: True or False: SQL table partitioning improves query performance only when the query's WHERE clause includes a filter on the partition key column.
questions.explanation: The statement is TRUE. SQL table partitioning — whether RANGE, LIST, or HASH — physically organises rows into sub-tables based on the partition key value. The query optimiser uses partition elimination (also called pruning) to skip entire sub-tables when a WHERE clause filters on the partition key, because it knows which partitions can contain matching rows. If the filter references a different column, the planner has no basis to exclude any partition and must scan all of them. This is why choosing the right partition key is critical: it must match the dominant filter predicate of real workloads. Secondary indexes, Z-ordering (Databricks), or data skipping statistics on non-partition columns can partially compensate, but they operate within partitions rather than skipping them at the folder-listing level.

questions.stem: A dbt model joins and transforms 600 million rows and is referenced by 50 downstream models and 5 BI dashboards. It is currently materialised as a `view`. What change should the team make and why?
options: Switch to `ephemeral` so the logic compiles as a CTE in each consumer's SQL, reducing the number of warehouse objects
Switch to `table` or `incremental` — a view re-executes the 600M-row scan on every downstream reference, so persisting the result eliminates redundant full scans
Switch to `incremental` and set `full_refresh: true` so that every run stays perfectly synchronised with the source table
Keep it as a `view` and add dbt data quality tests — materialisation choice does not affect query performance
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. A dbt view is a saved SELECT statement — every downstream query re-executes the full 600M-row scan independently. With 50 downstream models and 5 dashboards, the same expensive transformation runs dozens of times per refresh cycle. Materialising as a table or incremental model computes the result once and persists it, so consumers read from an already-computed object. Option A (ephemeral) makes this dramatically worse: ephemeral models compile as CTEs inlined into each consumer's SQL, meaning the 600M-row scan runs once per consumer per query rather than once globally. Option C misuses full_refresh: setting it permanently defeats the purpose of incremental loading, forcing a full rebuild every run with no benefit. Option D is factually wrong: materialisation choice is one of the most significant levers for dbt performance and cost.

questions.stem: True or False: In a dbt project, `{{ ref('staging_orders') }}` resolves the model's physical warehouse name at compile time AND adds a dependency edge from the current model to staging_orders in the project DAG.
questions.explanation: The statement is TRUE. The ref() function in dbt serves two equally important purposes. First, at compile time dbt replaces {{ ref('staging_orders') }} with the fully qualified warehouse object name for the active target environment (e.g., analytics_dev.staging_orders in development, analytics_prod.staging_orders in production), so engineers never hard-code environment-specific schema or database names. Second, dbt parses every ref() call across the entire project to build its directed acyclic graph of model dependencies. An edge from the current model to staging_orders tells dbt's runner to execute staging_orders before the current model. This DAG is also what powers commands like dbt run --select +my_model (run all ancestors) or state:modified+ (run changed models plus their descendants). The model referenced does not need to be pre-materialized before the run — dbt's execution order is derived entirely from this graph.

questions.stem: Which dbt generic test asserts that every value in a column exists in a specified column of another model — the equivalent of a soft foreign-key check?
options: not_null
unique
accepted_values
relationships
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D — relationships. The relationships generic test checks referential integrity: for every non-null value in the specified column of the model under test, it asserts that the value also exists in the referenced column of another model (typically a primary key). This is described as a 'soft' foreign-key check because dbt runs it as a query and logs failures rather than blocking writes, unlike a database-enforced FOREIGN KEY constraint. Option A (not_null) verifies that a column contains no NULL values. Option B (unique) verifies that all values in a column are distinct. Option C (accepted_values) checks that every value belongs to a hard-coded allowed list defined in schema.yml. When the relationships test fails in CI, it typically means a dimension table was not loaded before the fact table, or records were deleted from the parent table without cascading to children.

questions.stem: A CI pipeline runs `dbt run --select state:modified+`. A developer has modified only `stg_orders.sql`. Which models will dbt run?
options: Only `stg_orders` — state:modified selects exactly the changed model
`stg_orders` and all models that are downstream of it in the DAG
All models in the project whose SQL contains the string 'orders'
`stg_orders` and all models that were included in the previous successful CI run
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. The state:modified selector compares the current project against a previously compiled state artifact (typically production's manifest.json) and selects models whose compiled SQL or configuration has changed. The + suffix is dbt's graph operator meaning 'plus all downstream nodes' — it expands the selection to include every model that depends on stg_orders, directly or transitively, through the full depth of the DAG. The purpose is to catch downstream breakage: if stg_orders renames a column or changes a join, the models built on top of it may fail silently without this expanded selection. Option A describes state:modified without the + operator. Option C describes a text-search approach dbt does not use — selectors are graph-based, not string-based. Option D is unrelated to dbt's state functionality, which compares against a specific compiled artifact, not execution history.

questions.stem: True or False: A dbt `ephemeral` model creates a temporary table in the data warehouse that exists only for the duration of the dbt run and is dropped automatically when the run completes.
questions.explanation: The statement is FALSE. A dbt ephemeral model is never materialized as any physical object in the warehouse — not a table, not a view, and not a temporary table. Instead, dbt compiles the ephemeral model's SQL into a Common Table Expression (CTE) and inlines it directly into the SQL of every downstream model that references it via ref(). The ephemeral model exists only at compile time within dbt's process; the SQL sent to the warehouse contains a WITH clause embedding the logic. This means ephemeral models do not appear in the warehouse's object catalog and cannot be queried directly outside of a dbt run. They are suited for lightweight intermediate logic shared across a small number of consumers. The key trade-off: because the CTE is inlined, the ephemeral model's computation is re-executed once per downstream consumer per query — making it expensive for heavy transformations referenced frequently.

questions.stem: Before MCP, connecting N AI models to M external tools required how many custom integrations, and what does MCP reduce this to?
options: N + M integrations; MCP reduces this to N or M (whichever is smaller)
N × M integrations; MCP reduces this to N + M — one client per model, one server per tool
M integrations, because tools expose generic REST APIs that any AI model can call without custom code
N × M integrations; MCP eliminates tool calls entirely by replacing them with retrieval-augmented context
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Before MCP, every AI model that needed to call an external tool required a custom integration: its own authentication logic, API client, error handling, and data formatting specific to that tool. With N models and M tools, that produces N × M unique codebases to build, maintain, and update whenever either side changes. MCP solves this with a standard protocol: each AI model (or rather, the host application running it) implements one MCP client, and each tool provider implements one MCP server. Total integration surface shrinks to N + M. Option A mischaracterises the reduction — the point is eliminating cross-product duplication, not halving it. Option C is false — generic REST does not exist across tools; each has unique auth schemes, data shapes, and error models. Option D is false — MCP standardises tool calling, it does not replace tools with retrieval.

questions.stem: A team builds a Claude-based assistant that can query a Databricks metastore, call the dbt Cloud API, and write to Confluence. In MCP architecture, what is the correct description of these three capabilities?
options: Each capability should be a separate MCP Host; Claude is the Client that connects to all three
Each capability is a Tool primitive exposed by an MCP Server; Claude (via the Host) invokes them through the Client
Databricks and dbt are Resources because they return data; Confluence is a Tool because it involves writing
All three are Prompt primitives because the assistant needs pre-written instructions for interacting with each system
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. In MCP architecture, capabilities that an AI assistant can invoke — whether reading from Databricks, calling an API, or writing to Confluence — are Tool primitives exposed by MCP Servers. The Host (the application running Claude, such as Claude Desktop or a custom app) holds the conversation context and decides when to invoke a tool. The Client (embedded in the Host) communicates with MCP Servers over the transport protocol. Option A is wrong: the Host is the runtime application, not a per-capability construct; you don't create separate Hosts for each integration. Option C applies an incorrect read/write distinction — Resources in MCP are data exposed passively for the LLM's context, while Tools perform actions; both Databricks queries and Confluence writes are actions and therefore Tools. Option D is wrong: Prompts are pre-written instruction templates that the server offers; they are not the correct primitive for runtime callable capabilities.

questions.stem: True or False: MCP Tools are stateful — each tool call automatically carries context from all previous tool calls in the same session so the server can reference prior results.
questions.explanation: The statement is FALSE. MCP Tools are designed to be stateless. Each tool call is self-contained: the Host includes all necessary inputs in the call payload, and the Server returns a result without retaining any memory between invocations. This is a deliberate architectural choice — stateless servers are easier to scale horizontally, simpler to restart on failure, and prevent hidden coupling between tool calls that would make reasoning about the system difficult. Conversation context, intermediate results, and memory of prior tool calls all live in the MCP Host application layer, not in the Server. If an MCP workflow needs to pass the result of one tool call into a subsequent call, the Host is responsible for extracting that result and including it in the next call's parameters. Understanding this distinction is important for debugging multi-step AI workflows where state management is a common source of errors.

questions.stem: A security-conscious team is deploying an MCP server that exposes their Azure SQL database to an AI coding assistant. Which practice is MOST critical for safe deployment?
options: Use SSE transport instead of stdio to prevent the AI process from reading environment variables on the host machine
Grant the MCP server only the minimum database permissions needed (e.g., read-only on specific schemas), and authenticate using OAuth 2.1
Rotate the database password every 24 hours and inject a fresh credential into the MCP server configuration each morning
Run the MCP server behind a corporate VPN to ensure the AI model cannot send data to external endpoints
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B — least-privilege combined with OAuth 2.1 authentication. An MCP server with broad database permissions becomes a high-value attack surface: prompt injection in the AI's input could manipulate it into issuing destructive queries within its permitted scope. Limiting permissions to exactly what is needed (read-only on specific schemas, no DDL, no cross-database access) contains the blast radius of any compromise. OAuth 2.1 is MCP's recommended authentication standard and provides auditable, scoped access tokens. Option A (SSE vs stdio transport) is a deployment topology choice, not a primary security control; environment variable exposure is not the dominant risk. Option C (daily password rotation) is operationally cumbersome and addresses credential theft, not authorization scope — a stolen short-lived token with broad permissions is still dangerous. Option D (VPN) reduces network exposure but does not prevent the AI from making authorized but harmful queries within its allowed scope.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST distinguishes an MCP Resource from an MCP Tool?
options: Resources are callable with input parameters and return structured output; Tools provide static, read-only reference data
Resources expose data for the LLM to use as context (read-only); Tools are invokable actions that can perform computation or have side effects
Resources are scoped to a single conversation turn and discarded after; Tools persist their outputs across sessions
Resources require OAuth 2.1 authentication; Tools can use any authentication mechanism the server supports
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. MCP defines three server primitives with distinct roles: Tools, Resources, and Prompts. A Resource is data the server exposes for the LLM to consume as context — think of it as a structured data source: a file's contents, the results of a database query pre-fetched for background reading, or an API response the model should incorporate into its reasoning. Resources flow data to the LLM passively. A Tool is an action the LLM can invoke at runtime; it can perform computation, call external APIs, write to databases, or have other side effects. Tools are called actively by the LLM via the Host. Option A reverses the definitions entirely. Option C is wrong: neither Resources nor Tools are designed to persist state across sessions — MCP is stateless by design. Option D is false: authentication is a deployment concern, not a primitive-type constraint; both Resources and Tools can require authentication.

questions.stem: A data team's Jira project has 400 tickets; 250 have not been updated in more than six months. At sprint planning the team cannot tell which tickets are still relevant. What should they do FIRST?
options: Increase sprint capacity by adding team members so the old tickets can be closed more quickly
Run a backlog grooming session to archive or close irrelevant tickets before planning the next sprint
Use a Jira automation rule to close all tickets older than 90 days automatically
Convert all outstanding tickets to Epics so they no longer appear in the sprint backlog view
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. A stale backlog where 62% of tickets are untouched for six months is a prioritisation crisis, not a capacity crisis. Sprint planning becomes unreliable because the team cannot distinguish genuinely relevant work from abandoned ideas, resolved problems, or superseded requirements. The correct first step is a grooming (or refinement) session: deliberate human review to archive irrelevant tickets, re-estimate still-valid ones, and establish a manageable active backlog. Option A (add team members) does not fix the underlying problem — more people executing unvalidated work faster makes things worse. Option C (auto-close by age) is risky because some deferred tickets may represent legitimate future work; blanket automation destroys context without judgment. Option D (convert to Epics) hides tickets from sprint views without actually resolving the ambiguity about what is worth doing — it moves the problem, not solves it.

questions.stem: True or False: In Jira's agile issue hierarchy, an Epic can span multiple sprints, but a Story should be completed within a single sprint.
questions.explanation: The statement is TRUE. Jira's agile hierarchy is designed around predictable delivery cadences. An Epic represents a large body of work — a feature, a project phase, or a goal — that typically spans multiple sprints and may take weeks or months to complete. Stories (User Stories) are sized to fit within a single sprint, which is a core agile principle: if a story cannot be finished within the sprint, it should be broken down into smaller stories or moved back to the backlog for re-estimation. Tasks and Sub-tasks sit below Stories for finer-grained work tracking. While Jira technically allows a story to remain open across sprint boundaries, doing so repeatedly signals poor estimation and erodes the team's velocity reliability and sprint commitment. Epics serve as the umbrella that groups related stories and tracks progress toward a larger delivery milestone.

questions.stem: A saved Jira filter contains: `project = DATA AND status = "In Progress" AND assignee = currentUser()`. What does `currentUser()` evaluate to when the filter is opened?
options: The Jira project administrator's account
The service account used by active Jira automation rules in the project
The account of the person opening and running the filter at that moment
The user who most recently modified any issue in the project
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. currentUser() is a dynamic JQL function that evaluates to the Jira account of the person executing the query at the moment it runs — not a static user hardcoded at filter creation time. This makes it invaluable for shared filters and dashboard gadgets: every team member who opens the same saved filter sees only their own In Progress tickets, even though the filter definition is identical for all users. Option A (project administrator) is wrong — the admin's account is not implicitly associated with query execution. Option B (automation service account) is wrong — automation rules run under a service account, but currentUser() in a standard JQL query is evaluated in the context of the human user running the query, not any background process. Option D describes a different concept altogether. Understanding currentUser() prevents a common mistake where a developer hardcodes their own username and accidentally shares a filter that only shows their work to everyone.

questions.stem: A data team is about to build a new Power BI dashboard for the finance department. In which order should they use Figma and Jira to minimise rework?
options: Build the full dashboard in Power BI → present to finance → mark the Jira ticket done → document the layout in Figma afterwards
Write the Jira story with acceptance criteria → create a low-fidelity Figma wireframe → get stakeholder sign-off on the layout → build in Power BI
Create a high-fidelity Figma prototype using real data → present it as the near-final product → skip Jira for BI work since it is not software engineering
Open a Jira ticket → build the dashboard immediately → use Figma only if stakeholders request visual changes after delivery
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. The correct sequence is: capture requirements in a Jira story (with acceptance criteria) → validate the layout with a low-fidelity Figma wireframe → get stakeholder sign-off before building. This sequence exists because design alignment is cheapest when it is earliest. A Figma wireframe takes hours to revise; a Power BI report with a built-out data model, DAX measures, and formatted visuals can take days to restructure after stakeholder feedback. Option A (build first, document after) is the most expensive path — rework after delivery is the norm, not the exception. Option C (high-fidelity prototype first) conflates a design artefact with the deliverable and front-loads expensive design effort before requirements are confirmed; also, skipping Jira removes the audit trail and acceptance criteria. Option D (build immediately, use Figma only for complaints) is the second most common failure mode: it skips alignment and uses Figma reactively for damage control.

questions.stem: True or False: A Confluence page's edit history is a sufficient substitute for a 'last verified' date stamp, because readers can check when the page was last changed to assess whether the content is current.
questions.explanation: The statement is FALSE. A Confluence page's edit history records when the page was changed, not whether its content was reviewed and confirmed as accurate. A page can go unedited for 18 months because nobody noticed it was outdated — the edit history shows it as 'unchanged,' which superficially looks like stability but actually represents a staleness risk. A 'last verified' date stamp (implemented as a page property, a dated note at the top, or a page review workflow) signals that a human deliberately read the content on a specific date and confirmed it remained accurate even if no text changes were needed. This distinction matters in a data platform context where Confluence often holds runbooks, onboarding guides, and SLA documentation that can silently become dangerous when infrastructure changes. Without explicit ownership signals, readers cannot distinguish a well-maintained page from an abandoned one — a fundamental failure mode of unmanaged wikis.

questions.stem: A legacy analytics team stores and processes all data on a single on-premises SQL Server. At month-end reporting, query performance collapses under load; the rest of the month, half the capacity sits idle. Which principle of the modern data stack most directly resolves this structural inefficiency?
options: Implementing a data mesh to distribute ownership across business domains
Separating storage from compute so each tier can scale independently
Adopting an ELT pattern to delay transformation until query time
Moving to a NoSQL document store to remove schema constraints
questions.explanation: The modern data stack emerged specifically to solve the coupled scaling problem of monolithic warehouses. When storage and compute are fused on a single server, you must over-provision both to handle peak load — wasting money during off-peak periods. Storage/compute separation (e.g., ADLS + Azure Synapse serverless, or S3 + Snowflake) lets compute scale to zero when idle and burst when needed, while storage is billed purely on volume. Data mesh (option A) addresses ownership and governance, not scaling costs. ELT (option C) is a transformation sequencing choice, not a scaling architecture. NoSQL (option D) solves schema flexibility problems, not compute cost spikes. The scaling pain of monolithic warehouses is the founding problem the modern data stack was built to fix.

questions.stem: True or False: In the modern data stack, the ingestion layer is responsible for transforming raw source data into business-ready analytical models.
questions.explanation: The ingestion layer's job is to reliably move data from source systems into cloud storage — tools like Azure Data Factory, Fivetran, and Airbyte operate here. Transformation happens in a separate downstream layer, typically using tools like dbt, Spark, or SQL stored procedures. Confusing ingestion with transformation is one of the most common misconceptions for new data practitioners. The 'L' in ELT stands for Load — you load raw data first, then transform it in place using the power of the cloud warehouse. Ingestion tools are intentionally kept simple: they copy data faithfully, without business logic, so that transformations can be tested, versioned, and replayed independently. A team that embeds complex transformation logic in their ingestion pipelines creates brittle, hard-to-test pipelines that break when business rules change.

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes the four fundamental layers of a modern data platform?
options: Source systems → Transformation → Storage → Presentation
Ingestion → Storage → Transformation → Presentation
Extraction → Staging → Processing → Reporting
Collection → Streaming → Warehousing → Analysis
questions.explanation: Every modern data platform follows the same logical flow: (1) Ingestion — move data from sources into cloud storage; (2) Storage — persist raw and processed data cheaply at scale; (3) Transformation — apply business logic to convert raw data into analytics-ready models; (4) Presentation — serve those models to end users via BI tools, APIs, or notebooks. Option A skips ingestion and inverts the ELT order by putting transformation before storage. Option C conflates ETL-era terminology with modern patterns and omits the ingestion stage. Option D mixes streaming (one specific ingestion pattern) with the remaining layers, creating a false taxonomy. Understanding this four-layer model helps practitioners see where tools like ADF, ADLS, dbt, and Power BI each belong in the broader architecture.

questions.stem: A Director is evaluating Azure Synapse Analytics Dedicated SQL Pool versus Databricks for a petabyte-scale workload that requires both batch ETL and exploratory ML by data scientists. Which statement most accurately differentiates these platforms for this combined workload?
options: Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool supports Python natively, making it preferable for ML; Databricks is SQL-only
Databricks runs Apache Spark natively and supports Python and Scala for ML; Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool is an MPP SQL warehouse optimised for structured query patterns
Both platforms are equivalent because both support Delta Lake and can run Spark jobs
Synapse integrates directly with Azure DevOps while Databricks requires third-party CI/CD tooling
questions.explanation: Synapse Analytics Dedicated SQL Pool is a Massively Parallel Processing (MPP) SQL warehouse descended from Azure SQL Data Warehouse — it excels at large-scale SQL analytics on structured data but is not a native environment for Python ML workflows. Databricks is built on Apache Spark, supports Python, Scala, R, and SQL natively, and provides MLflow integration, making it far better suited for mixed ETL-plus-ML teams. Option A is incorrect — Synapse Dedicated Pool does not natively support Python in its SQL engine. Option C misses the fundamental architectural difference: Databricks runs Spark; Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool does not. Option D is incorrect — Databricks has robust Git and Azure DevOps integration via Asset Bundles. For a team combining ETL with data science, Databricks is the stronger fit; for pure SQL analytics, Synapse SQL Pool is competitive.

questions.stem: True or False: The modern data stack emerged primarily to enable data teams to replace proprietary databases with open-source alternatives.
questions.explanation: While open-source tooling (dbt, Apache Spark, Airflow) is a hallmark of the modern data stack, it is not the problem the stack was designed to solve. The primary driver was the inability of on-premises monolithic data warehouses to scale cost-effectively: storage and compute were tightly coupled, meaning teams had to provision for peak capacity at all times, paying for idle resources. The modern data stack solved this by separating storage (cheap, infinitely scalable) from compute (elastic, billed per use). Open-source adoption was a consequence of cloud-native architectures and community momentum, not the founding pain. A practitioner who conflates the 'why' (scalability and cost) with the 'what' (open-source tooling) will struggle to make principled architectural trade-offs when proprietary tools are the better fit for the problem.

questions.stem: In Apache Spark's architecture, which component is responsible for translating user code into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of stages and tasks, and then negotiating resources from the Cluster Manager?
options: Executor
Worker Node
Driver
Shuffle Manager
questions.explanation: The Driver is the brain of every Spark application. It runs the user's main() function or notebook code, builds the logical and physical execution plan, converts it into a DAG of stages, and requests executor slots from the Cluster Manager (YARN, Kubernetes, or Databricks' own manager). Executors (option A) receive task assignments from the Driver and run them on data partitions — they do not plan. Worker Nodes (option B) are the physical machines that host executors; they don't interpret user code. The Shuffle Manager is an internal component that handles data redistribution between stages during joins and aggregations, not DAG creation or resource negotiation. Understanding the Driver/Executor split also explains why calling .collect() can cause a Driver out-of-memory error — all data is pulled to that single node.

questions.stem: True or False: In Apache Spark, calling a transformation like .filter() or .select() on a DataFrame immediately triggers data processing across the cluster.
questions.explanation: Spark uses a lazy evaluation model. Transformations — filter, select, join, groupBy, withColumn — build a logical execution plan but execute no computation when called. Execution is deferred until an action is triggered: .count(), .collect(), .write(), or .show(). At action time, Spark's Catalyst optimizer reviews the full logical plan, applies optimizations (predicate pushdown, join reordering), generates a physical plan, and executes it across the cluster. This laziness enables whole-plan optimization that would be impossible if each operation ran immediately. For practitioners debugging Spark performance, this means timing lines like df.filter(...) is meaningless — only the action at the end of the chain triggers real computation and takes measurable time. Benchmarking transformations in isolation will always show near-zero elapsed time.

questions.stem: A Databricks team needs to continuously ingest new Parquet files landing in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 without writing custom file-tracking logic or polling loops. Which Databricks feature is purpose-built for this pattern?
options: Delta Live Tables
Unity Catalog
Auto Loader
Asset Bundles
questions.explanation: Auto Loader (the cloudFiles source in Spark Structured Streaming) is Databricks' managed incremental file ingestion tool. It monitors ADLS, S3, or GCS for new files using cloud event notifications or directory listing, maintains a checkpoint of processed files automatically, and handles schema inference and evolution. Delta Live Tables (option A) is a declarative framework for building multi-hop ETL pipelines — it can use Auto Loader internally but is not the ingestion mechanism itself. Unity Catalog (option B) is a governance and cataloging layer, not an ingestion feature. Asset Bundles (option D) is Databricks' CI/CD packaging and deployment tool for Workflows and jobs. Auto Loader specifically solves 'ingest new cloud files at scale without custom state management' — a foundational requirement for any streaming or near-real-time Lakehouse pipeline.

questions.stem: Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace to address data assets in Databricks. Which of the following correctly represents this hierarchy?
options: Workspace.Database.Table
Metastore.Schema.Table
Catalog.Schema.Table
Organization.Catalog.Dataset
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog's three-level namespace is Catalog.Schema.Table — every asset is addressed as catalog_name.schema_name.table_name (e.g., prod.finance.transactions). This replaces the legacy Hive two-level namespace (database.table) and allows governance boundaries at the catalog level. The Metastore sits above the entire namespace as the top-level Unity Catalog object containing all catalogs, but it is not part of the three-level addressing scheme. Option A uses 'Workspace' and 'Database,' which are legacy Databricks concepts that Unity Catalog supersedes. Option B incorrectly promotes Metastore to the first namespace level. Option D describes a fictional hierarchy. In practice, catalogs typically represent environments (dev, staging, prod) or major domains, while schemas hold related tables and views within a domain.

questions.stem: True or False: Databricks' Photon engine replaces Spark's JVM-based execution layer entirely, so PySpark code must be rewritten in C++ to take advantage of Photon's performance gains.
questions.explanation: Photon is Databricks' vectorized query engine written in C++, designed to accelerate SQL and DataFrame operations beyond what the JVM-based Tungsten engine achieves. However, it operates transparently beneath the existing Spark API — PySpark, Scala Spark, and SQL code runs completely unchanged. Photon intercepts eligible operations at the physical plan level and executes them in its optimized C++ runtime without any changes from the user. Operations Photon does not yet support fall back silently to the standard Spark JVM engine. Teams enable Photon with a single cluster configuration toggle and typically see 2–10× speedups on scan-heavy and aggregation-heavy workloads with zero code migration. The misconception that Photon requires code rewriting reflects a misunderstanding of where Photon sits in the execution stack — it is an engine swap underneath the API, not an API replacement.

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to inspect what new commits have appeared on the remote main branch since their last sync, without automatically merging those changes into their local branch. Which git command achieves this?
options: git pull origin main
git merge origin/main
git checkout origin/main
git fetch origin main
questions.explanation: git fetch origin main downloads remote objects and refs but does not modify any local branch or the working directory. After fetching, the engineer can run git log origin/main or git diff HEAD origin/main to review changes before deciding to merge. git pull origin main (option A) fetches and then automatically merges into the current local branch — it modifies local state without a review step. git merge origin/main (option B) merges using the last-fetched remote-tracking branch; without a prior fetch, it uses stale cached data. git checkout origin/main (option C) puts the working directory in detached HEAD state at the remote's commit — useful for inspecting a specific state, but not for the described fetch-then-review workflow. Fetch-then-inspect is the professional pattern before integrating remote changes into active work.

questions.stem: True or False: Running git rebase main on a feature branch rewrites the feature branch's commit history, which can cause problems if the branch has already been pushed to a shared remote repository.
questions.explanation: git rebase replays commits from the current branch onto a new base, generating new commit SHA hashes even when the content is unchanged. This rewrites history. If teammates have already fetched or cloned the original feature branch commits, their local history will diverge from the rebased version after a force-push. They will face confusing merge conflicts or orphaned commits when they try to pull the rebased branch. The golden rule of rebasing is: never rebase a branch that others are working from. For data engineering teams, rebase is safe only on purely local, unshared branches. Once a branch is pushed and under review (e.g., a PR is open), use merge instead — it creates an explicit merge commit but preserves shared history. The linear history that rebase produces is aesthetically cleaner but not worth the team disruption of shared-branch rewrites.

questions.stem: A junior data engineer is working in Azure Data Factory with Git integration enabled. After testing a pipeline on a feature branch, they click the 'Publish' button in the ADF Studio UI. Why is this considered a serious mistake in Git-integrated ADF workflows?
options: The Publish button is disabled in Git mode and clicking it generates an error
Publishing in Git mode bypasses the CI/CD pipeline and deploys the ARM template directly to the live ADF service, outside any approval or testing gates
The Publish button automatically merges the feature branch into the collaboration branch, skipping pull request review
Publishing deletes the feature branch from the remote repository once deployment completes
questions.explanation: In ADF Git mode the Publish button deploys the current collaboration branch's ARM template directly to the live ADF service — bypassing any CI/CD pipeline, approval gates, or integration tests the team has configured in GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps. The 'publish' operation and the 'Git merge' workflow are two completely separate mechanisms: a developer can accidentally publish a feature branch mid-development, or publish changes that have never been reviewed. Microsoft's own guidance recommends disabling the Publish button for individual developers and allowing only a service principal or release pipeline to publish. Option A is incorrect — the button is fully active in Git mode, which is precisely the danger. Option C describes what a PR merge does, not what Publish does. Option D is false — Publish has no effect on branch state.

questions.stem: A data engineering team follows GitHub Flow. A critical bug in a production dbt model is discovered on a Friday afternoon and must be fixed immediately. What is the correct approach under GitHub Flow?
options: Commit the fix directly to main because urgency overrides branching conventions
Create a hotfix branch from main, commit the fix, push it, open a pull request, get rapid approval, and merge
Create a long-lived release/hotfix branch, apply the fix, then schedule a merge to main during next week's release window
Revert main to the last known-good tag and redeploy, then address the fix in the next sprint
questions.explanation: GitHub Flow's core rule is that main is always deployable and all changes — including urgent hotfixes — go through a short-lived branch and pull request cycle. Even for a critical fix, the workflow is: create a branch from main, commit the fix, push, open a PR, request expedited review, merge, and deploy. A simple fix can be reviewed and approved in minutes. Skipping the PR removes the second pair of eyes that catches table name typos, wrong column references, or incomplete fixes under time pressure. Option A (direct commit to main) violates the foundational GitHub Flow rule and bypasses peer review. Option C describes Gitflow's release-branch pattern, which GitHub Flow deliberately avoids. Option D (revert and defer) leaves a known bug in production while deprioritizing the fix — a worse outcome than a fast PR.

questions.stem: True or False: Adding a file to .gitignore will stop Git from tracking that file, even if Git has already been tracking it in a previous commit.
questions.explanation: .gitignore only prevents untracked files from being staged by git add. If a file is already tracked — meaning it has appeared in at least one prior commit — adding it to .gitignore has no effect. Git will continue tracking changes to that file and will include it in future commits. To stop tracking an already-committed file, the engineer must run git rm --cached <filename> to remove it from the index, then add it to .gitignore, then commit the removal. This is a common trap for data engineers who add .env or credentials.json to .gitignore after accidentally committing secrets — the file remains in history and in future diffs until explicitly un-tracked. If the repo is shared or public, a history rewrite (git filter-repo or BFG) is required to expunge the sensitive file from all historical commits.

questions.stem: A legal tech firm wants to automatically extract all company names, contract dates, and monetary values from thousands of PDF contracts. Which NLP task type best describes this requirement?
options: Text classification
Abstractive summarization
Named Entity Recognition (NER)
Conversational AI
questions.explanation: Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies and classifies spans of text into predefined categories such as ORGANIZATION, DATE, MONEY, and LOCATION. Extracting 'company names, contract dates, and monetary values' is precisely this task — each target is a typed entity span within unstructured prose. Text classification (option A) assigns a whole document to a category (e.g., 'is this a services contract or a goods contract?') — it does not extract structured values. Abstractive summarization (option B) generates a condensed version of the document and cannot reliably extract specific structured fields. Conversational AI (option C) is designed for dialogue turns, not document parsing. NER is the foundational task for legal tech, financial document processing, and knowledge extraction workflows — it transforms unstructured prose into structured records that a database can consume downstream.

questions.stem: True or False: Extractive summarization carries a lower risk of factual hallucination than abstractive summarization because it selects and assembles existing sentences from the source document rather than generating new text.
questions.explanation: Extractive summarization copies sentences or phrases verbatim from the source document — the output is guaranteed to exist in the input, so the model cannot invent facts that are not there. Abstractive summarization (what modern LLMs produce) generates new text that aims to convey the same meaning. This generation process is statistical: the model predicts plausible next tokens, not factually verified ones, so it can produce fluent sentences that contradict or misrepresent the source. This structural difference matters in high-stakes domains: a legal or medical summary produced by abstractive generation may sound authoritative while containing errors. For applications where factual accuracy is paramount and verbatim fidelity is acceptable — contract review, clinical notes, regulatory filings — extractive methods or RAG with strict citation are safer than pure generative summarization.

questions.stem: A customer support team evaluates an NLP classifier that flags tickets requiring escalation. The model achieves precision = 0.94 and recall = 0.38 on the escalation class. What does this mean operationally for the team?
options: The model correctly identifies almost all escalation tickets but generates many false alarms
The model almost never flags a ticket incorrectly as an escalation, but it misses roughly 62% of tickets that genuinely require it
The model has high overall accuracy, meaning it handles most tickets correctly
The model should be retrained because high precision always indicates overfitting on the training set
questions.explanation: Precision = 0.94 means that when the model flags a ticket for escalation, it is correct 94% of the time — very few false positives. Recall = 0.38 means the model catches only 38% of all tickets that genuinely need escalation, missing 62% of true escalation cases. The operational consequence: most real escalation tickets slip through to frontline agents who aren't equipped to handle them. This is the opposite of option A, which describes high recall and high precision. Option C conflates precision with accuracy — a model that labels everything 'not escalation' would have high accuracy on an imbalanced dataset but precision and recall of 0 on the minority class. Option D misinterprets precision; it measures prediction quality on positive predictions, not overfitting. In escalation routing, missed escalations (low recall) typically cause more harm than false alarms (low precision), since a misrouted ticket harms the customer directly.

questions.stem: A business needs to automatically flag support tickets containing toxic or abusive language. A labeled dataset of 50,000 tickets (5% toxic) is available. Which NLP task type and model approach is most appropriate?
options: Named Entity Recognition using a rules-based grammar to match known offensive words
Binary text classification with a fine-tuned transformer model, evaluated using F1 score on the toxic class
Abstractive summarization to condense each ticket before manual review
Conversational AI with RAG to generate a toxicity recommendation from retrieved examples
questions.explanation: Flagging toxic language is a binary text classification problem — each ticket is toxic or not. With 50,000 labeled examples, fine-tuning a pre-trained transformer (e.g., BERT or RoBERTa) is the standard approach: the model already understands language from pre-training and adapts quickly to domain-specific toxicity patterns. F1 on the minority (toxic) class is the right evaluation metric for a 5%/95% imbalanced dataset — overall accuracy would be misleading because a model that always predicts 'not toxic' achieves 95% accuracy while catching zero abusive tickets. Option A (rules-based NER) cannot generalize beyond a fixed offensive-word vocabulary and misses euphemisms, context-dependent toxicity, and novel language patterns. Option C (summarization) does not automate flagging at all. Option D (RAG-based conversational AI) is architecturally mismatched — retrieval-augmented generation is designed for open-ended generation tasks, not binary classification.

questions.stem: True or False: Hallucination in large language models is fundamentally a training data quality problem; with sufficiently clean and accurate fine-tuning data, hallucination can be eliminated.
questions.explanation: Hallucination is a structural property of autoregressive language models, not a data quality defect. LLMs generate text by predicting the next token based on probability distributions learned during training — they optimize for plausibility, not factual accuracy. Even a model trained exclusively on verified, correct text will hallucinate, because generation has no ground-truth lookup mechanism: it interpolates from learned statistical patterns, and those patterns break down in novel or underrepresented combinations. Fine-tuning on cleaner data reduces hallucination frequency but does not eliminate it. Effective mitigation uses architectural patterns — RAG to ground generation in retrieved documents, citation enforcement, output verification pipelines, and Constitutional AI techniques — that shift rather than remove the structural risk. Teams expecting zero hallucination from 'better training data' build on a false premise; robust architectures must treat hallucination as a residual risk and add verification layers accordingly.

questions.stem:
A data engineering team is configuring a GitHub Actions workflow to run dbt tests on every pull request. A new team member asks why the workflow file must be in YAML rather than JSON, since their CI tool supports both.

Which reason most accurately explains why YAML is preferred for human-authored configuration files in modern data tooling?
options: YAML files parse faster than JSON at runtime, so using YAML reduces pipeline startup latency.
YAML supports inline comments, multi-line string blocks, and uses indentation instead of brackets — making intent clearer to the author without adding syntax noise.
YAML is a strict superset of JSON, so any existing JSON configuration automatically becomes valid YAML with no changes required.
YAML schemas are enforced by default on all major CI/CD platforms, preventing misconfiguration before a pipeline ever runs.
questions.explanation: YAML became the dominant configuration format because it optimises for human readability. It allows inline comments (using #), supports multi-line block strings with the literal (|) and folded (>) operators, and uses indentation rather than curly braces and commas — reducing visual noise in files developers read daily. JSON requires explicit quoting for all keys and strings, forbids comments, and becomes hard to scan at depth. While YAML is technically a superset of JSON — meaning valid JSON is also valid YAML — this is not why practitioners prefer it for hand-authored configs. Runtime parsing speed is negligible in CI contexts. Schema enforcement is optional (via yamllint) and is not a built-in platform feature. Practical takeaway: choose YAML for any configuration that humans author or read frequently; use JSON for machine-to-machine data interchange.

questions.stem: Statement: Any valid JSON document is also valid YAML, because YAML is designed as a superset of JSON and its specification guarantees that well-formed JSON can be parsed by any compliant YAML parser.
questions.explanation: TRUE. The YAML 1.2 specification explicitly positions YAML as a superset of JSON — any well-formed JSON document is valid YAML and produces the same logical data structure when parsed. This means teams can rename a .json config to .yml and incrementally add YAML-specific features (comments, anchors, multi-line strings) without reformatting existing content. The relationship is one-directional: YAML's indentation-based block syntax has no JSON equivalent. Minor edge cases exist around certain JSON float representations and the null keyword, but these do not violate the specification. Understanding this superset relationship matters when migrating tool configurations: an ADF linked-service JSON file can serve as a starting point for a YAML-based config file without requiring full reformatting.

questions.stem:
A data engineer is writing a dbt schema YAML file. One column's description must include a multi-line SQL snippet where every newline must be preserved exactly as written — including the line breaks between SELECT, FROM, and WHERE clauses.

Which YAML scalar style is correct for this requirement?
options: Flow scalars using JSON-style double-quoted strings with \n escape sequences embedded throughout.
Literal block scalars using the pipe (|) operator, which preserves newlines exactly as written.
Folded block scalars using the greater-than (>) operator, which replaces newlines with spaces.
Plain scalars without any quoting, letting the YAML parser decide how to handle embedded whitespace.
questions.explanation: The literal block scalar style (|) is correct when newlines must be preserved verbatim. Everything indented beneath the pipe operator is treated as a multi-line string with each line break retained exactly — ideal for SQL snippets, Markdown, or any structured text with meaningful formatting. The folded style (>) collapses single newlines into spaces and only preserves a line break when two consecutive newlines appear — suited for long prose that word-wraps, not for SQL. Flow scalars (double-quoted or single-quoted) require explicit \n escape sequences for newlines, making them error-prone for long blocks. Plain scalars are parsed as single-line strings and strip surrounding whitespace. In practice, dbt column descriptions containing backtick-formatted SQL or numbered lists should always use the pipe operator to ensure the rendered output is correctly formatted.

questions.stem: Which of the following data engineering tools uses YAML as the ONLY supported format for defining its automation — including job steps, triggers, and environment variables?
options: Azure Data Factory — pipeline definitions, linked services, and triggers are all authored in YAML files checked into source control.
Databricks Delta Live Tables — the pipeline graph of streaming and batch table dependencies is declared in YAML notebooks.
GitHub Actions — all workflow definitions are YAML files stored under .github/workflows/, specifying every job, step, and trigger.
dbt — model transformations, test logic, and materialisation strategies are all written in YAML inside the models/ directory.
questions.explanation: GitHub Actions uses YAML exclusively for workflow configuration — the .github/workflows/*.yml files define jobs, steps, on: triggers, runner environments, and secret references. There is no alternative format. Azure Data Factory serialises pipelines as JSON under the hood; the portal GUI and ARM templates use JSON, not YAML. Databricks Delta Live Tables uses Python or SQL notebook cells to declare the pipeline graph. dbt uses YAML for schema files, source definitions, and test configurations — but the actual transformations are SQL, not YAML. A data engineer setting up a dbt CI/CD pipeline will write YAML for the GitHub Actions workflow file AND YAML for the dbt schema.yml, but these serve different tools. Understanding which tool owns which format prevents misconfiguration when wiring up CI/CD for data projects.

questions.stem: Statement: YAML anchors (&) and aliases (*) allow a value to be defined once and reused multiple times within the same file, reducing duplication in configurations such as a dbt profiles.yml where multiple environments share a common base connection block.
questions.explanation: TRUE. YAML anchors and aliases solve the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) problem in configuration files. Define an anchor with &name on any node, and reference it later with *name to insert the anchored value verbatim at that location. The merge key (<<: *anchorName) allows an anchored mapping to be deep-merged into a new mapping, enabling inheritance-like patterns. In dbt's profiles.yml, a team can anchor a shared connection block (host, port, database name) and alias it across dev, staging, and prod targets — each target then only overrides its specific fields. One caveat: anchor support varies across YAML processors. Some CI runners, Kubernetes manifest tools, and certain Databricks YAML config parsers do not expand anchors or silently ignore them. Always verify anchor support in your specific tooling before relying on them in production configurations.

questions.stem:
